column1: Additional injunctions followed in other districts. A Louisiana federal court blocked the rule in Louisiana
column2: Mississippi
column3: Montana
column4: and Idaho. A federal court in Missouri covered Missouri
column5: Arkansas
column6: Iowa
column7: Nebraska
column8: North Dakota
column9: and South Dakota. An injunction in the Northern District of Alabama covered Alabama
column10: Florida
column11: Georgia
column12: and South Carolina. A Texas federal court issued an injunction covering Texas
column13: along with several Texas-based school districts that had separately challenged the rule. By the time the rule's effective date arrived
column14: enforcement was blocked across roughly half the country. The Sixth Circuit and other appellate courts declined to stay the injunctions pending appeal
column15: allowing the blocks to remain in place.

column1: The athletics provisions present a distinct complication. The Biden administration specifically carved transgender athletic participation out of the April 2024 final rule. A separate proposed rule on athletic eligibility
column2: issued in April 2023
column3: would have prohibited categorical bans on transgender student athletes but allowed schools to impose restrictions when they served important educational objectives and were narrowly tailored. That proposed rule never reached final form. The April 2024 rule therefore did not directly govern whether schools could restrict transgender girls from competing on girls sports teams. Schools remained subject to whatever state laws applied and to the Department's pre-existing policy positions
column4: which were themselves in legal limbo given the injunctions.

column1: The practical result by late 2024 was substantial regulatory uncertainty. Schools in states with injunctions operated under the pre-2024 regulatory framework while courts adjudicated the merits. Schools in states without injunctions faced a rule whose enforceability depended on litigation outcomes that remained unresolved. The Trump administration's return to office in January 2025 added another variable. The incoming administration announced it would not defend the Biden rule and would pursue rescission through new rulemaking. That process requires its own notice and comment period
column2:
meaning the regulatory landscape over Title IX and gender identity will remain contested well beyond the immediate litigation. The statute Congress wrote in 37 words in 1972 continues to generate legal conflicts that show no sign of reaching a stable resolution.

column1: CB-000219	"The No Fly List: How You Get On It
column2: Why You Can't Find Out
column3:
and How You Fight It" "no-fly-list-how-added-due-process-challenges-documented-record" "law_and_justice" "published" "91" "No Fly List How You Get Added and How You Challenge | CB-000219" "Latif v. Holder 9th Circuit (2014); Terrorist Screening Database watchlist process; DHS TRIP program documentation; DOJ OIG watchlist reports" "no fly list|due process|terrorism watchlist|ACLU|surveillance" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "The federal government maintains a no fly list that bars Americans from air travel. The process for being added is secret. The process for challenging it has been documented through litigation." "

The federal no fly list operates as one of the most consequential government databases in American life. A person placed on the list loses the ability to board commercial aircraft in or out of the United States. That person may never know they are on the list until they attempt to travel. They may never learn why they were placed there. And for years
column4: they had almost no meaningful path to challenge the designation. The legal and civil liberties debates surrounding this system have persisted for two decades
column5: producing court rulings
column6: congressional scrutiny
column7: and documented cases of profound government error.

column1: The Terrorist Screening Database
The no fly list is a subset of a larger government repository called the Terrorist Screening Database (TSDB)
column2: maintained by the FBI's Terrorist Screening Center. The TSDB consolidates terrorist identity information from multiple intelligence and law enforcement agencies into a single watchlist. The no fly list itself contains the names of individuals the government has determined pose a threat to aviation security specifically. A separate subset
column3: the selectee list
column4: subjects travelers to enhanced screening rather than outright prohibition.

column1: CB Factoid: A 2007 Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General audit titled ""The Federal Bureau of Investigation's Terrorist Watchlist Nomination Practices
column2: published September 2009
column3: found that the FBI failed to nominate known or suspected terrorists to the TSDB in a timely manner in 72 of 216 cases reviewed
column4: and that the watchlist contained records with outdated or incorrect information
column5: raising serious questions about both over-inclusion and under-inclusion errors (DOJ OIG Report I-2009-008
column6: September 2009).

column1: The precise size of the no fly list has varied over time and is not always publicly disclosed. Government documents and reporting have placed the list at various points between several thousand and tens of thousands of names. Leaked government documents published in 2014 revealed that the broader TSDB had grown to approximately 680
column2: 000 records
column3: with about 40 percent of those individuals having no recognized terrorist group affiliation. The no fly list itself contained roughly 47
column4: 000 names at that time
column5: including approximately 800 American citizens and legal permanent residents.

column1: The ""Reasonable Suspicion"" Standard

Placement on the no fly list does not require probable cause. It does not require criminal charges. It does not require a conviction. The legal standard for nomination is ""reasonable suspicion"" that a person is a known or suspected terrorist. That standard sits deliberately below probable cause
column2: the threshold required to obtain an arrest warrant or search warrant under the Fourth Amendment.

column1: The nomination process occurs within executive branch agencies and intelligence community components. Nominations go to the Terrorist Screening Center for review. The criteria for what constitutes reasonable suspicion in this context are not published in accessible form for the public or for the individuals being nominated. The government treats the specific bases for any individual nomination as classified or sensitive law enforcement information. This creates a system where a person can lose a fundamental liberty interest based on a secret determination
column2: made under a vague standard
column3: with no advance notice.

column1: The American Civil Liberties Union and other civil liberties organizations have argued for years that reasonable suspicion
column2: applied in secret without adversarial testing
column3: is an insufficient basis for denying Americans the ability to travel. Courts have largely agreed that more is required
column4: though the government has resisted providing it.

column1: DHS TRIP: The Official Redress Program
The Department of Homeland Security created the Traveler Redress Inquiry Program
column2: known as DHS TRIP
column3: as the official mechanism for individuals to challenge travel screening issues
column4: including potential no fly list placement. A person submits an inquiry
column5: the government reviews the matter internally
column6: and the government sends a determination letter. That letter confirms or denies nothing specific. It does not tell the applicant whether they are on the no fly list. It does not explain why any adverse determination was made. It does not provide the evidence or information the government relied upon.

column1: CB Factoid: The DHS TRIP program's own documentation
column2: published in the DHS Privacy Impact Assessment for the Traveler Redress Inquiry Program (DHS/TSA/PIA-018
column3: updated January 2016)
column4: confirms that the redress process does not disclose to applicants whether they appear in the TSDB or the no fly list
column5: and that final determination letters are standardized responses that do not explain the basis for any continued screening designation.

column1: In practice
column2: DHS TRIP gave affected travelers almost no actionable information. A person who submitted a TRIP inquiry and received a denial letter still did not know whether they were on the no fly list
column3: why they were there
column4: or what they could do to get off. The program satisfied a bureaucratic requirement for a redress mechanism without providing the substance that would make redress meaningful. Federal courts eventually found this constitutionally inadequate.

column1: Latif v. Holder: The Ninth Circuit's Ruling
The most significant judicial ruling on no fly list due process came from the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals in Latif v. Holder
column2: decided in 2014. The plaintiffs were American citizens and lawful permanent residents who had been placed on the no fly list and denied any meaningful opportunity to challenge their placement. They included veterans
column3: professors
column4: and a college student. Several had tried and failed to get answers through DHS TRIP.

column1: CB Factoid: In Latif v. Holder
column3: the Ninth Circuit held that the government's no fly list procedures violated the due process clause of the Fifth Amendment
column4: finding that the DHS TRIP redress process failed to provide plaintiffs with sufficient notice of the reasons for their placement or a meaningful opportunity to respond
column5: and ordered the government to develop new procedures that included notice and an opportunity to be heard before a neutral decisionmaker.

column1: The Ninth Circuit held that placement on the no fly list implicates a constitutionally protected liberty interest. The court found that the DHS TRIP process
column2: as it existed
column3: failed to provide constitutionally adequate process. The court required the government to give listed individuals notice that they are on the no fly list
column4: a summary of the reasons for their placement
column5: and a meaningful opportunity to contest that placement before a neutral decision-maker. The court did not require the government to disclose classified information in a way that would endanger national security
column6: but it held that the complete absence of notice and hearing was unconstitutional.

column1: Following the ruling
column2: the government revised its procedures to provide listed individuals with a letter confirming no fly list status and offering an in-person meeting with a DHS official. Critics noted that the revised process still fell short of full adversarial proceedings
column3: because the government retained the ability to rely on classified information the individual could not see or effectively challenge.

column1: Documented Cases of Erroneous Listings

The most vivid evidence of systemic problems with the no fly list comes from documented cases of clearly erroneous designations. The government has placed children on the list. Infants have been flagged at airport checkpoints because their names matched names in the database. Senator Ted Kennedy reported in 2004 that his name appeared on a watchlist and that he was stopped multiple times before staffers intervened to resolve the situation. A sitting United States senator required personal intervention from the Secretary of Homeland Security to fix a watchlist problem.
Veterans have appeared on the list.
column2: including veterans and other American citizens placed on the no fly list."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Among the plaintiffs in Latif v. Holder was Amir Meshal
column3: an American citizen who served no criminal sentence and faced no charges but found himself unable to fly home to the United States. Rahinah Ibrahim
column4: a Malaysian architect and academic who had studied and worked in the United States
column5: spent years litigating her no fly list placement and ultimately prevailed in court
column6: where the government acknowledged that an FBI agent had checked the wrong box on a nomination form.

column1: Common names create persistent problems. Individuals named David Nelson
column2: Robert Johnson
column3: or other frequently occurring names have reported repeated screening delays because their names generate watchlist hits that belong to someone else. The government has a ""known traveler"" system intended to help frequent fliers with common names
column4: but the underlying problem of over-inclusion persists.

column1: Racial and Religious Profiling

Documented evidence strongly suggests that Muslim Americans and Arab Americans appear on the no fly list at rates disproportionate to their share of the population. The 2014 Intercept documents revealed that among American citizens on the broader watchlist
column2: the proportion with Muslim or Arab names was striking. Advocacy organizations have collected testimony from hundreds of Muslim American travelers who describe consistent screening delays
column3: missed flights
column4: and secondary inspections.

column1: The ACLU's litigation and reporting has documented specific cases. Mohamed Ibrahim
column2: Faisal Nabin Kashem
column3: and other plaintiffs with Muslim names have described the professional and personal consequences of no fly list placement. Some lost jobs. Some could not visit dying relatives abroad. Some could not return home from overseas travel without extended government negotiations.

column1: The government has consistently declined to confirm or deny watchlist status in most individual cases
column2: making systematic analysis difficult. But research from the Brennan Center for Justice and the American Civil Liberties Union has assembled substantial evidence that nomination criteria
column3: applied through programs like the FBI's Domestic Investigations and Operations Guide
column4: tend to focus investigative resources on Muslim communities in ways that feed disproportionate watchlist nominations. The DOJ OIG report from 2009 noted that watchlist nomination standards were inconsistently applied across field offices
column5: creating conditions where bias at the nomination stage could influence database composition.

column1: The no fly list remains in operation. The government has made procedural adjustments in response to court orders. But the fundamental architecture of the system persists. A secret determination
column2: made under a sub-probable-cause standard
column3: still drives placement. The burden of challenging that placement still falls on the individual who lost the ability to travel. And the consequences of erroneous listing
column4: documented in courtrooms and congressional hearings alike
column5: continue to fall with striking regularity on Americans whose names
column6: faces
column7:
or faiths fit a particular profile.

column1:
CB-000220 "FISA Section 702: The Mass Surveillance Authority That Gets Renewed Every Few Years" "fisa-section-702-mass-surveillance-authority-documented-scope-reauthorization" "law_and_justice" "published" "91" "FISA Section 702 Surveillance Authority What It | CB-000220" "FISA Section 702 (50 U.S.C. § 1881a); PCLOB 2023 Section 702 report; ODNI transparency report 2024; FISC declassified opinions 2023" "FISA Section 702|surveillance|NSA|Fourth Amendment|privacy" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act authorizes collection of Americans' communications without a warrant if a foreign target is involved. Congress renewed it in 2024." "

Few surveillance authorities generate more legal controversy with less public understanding than Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act. The provision sits at the center of an ongoing argument about whether the United States government conducts mass surveillance of its own citizens under the legal cover of targeting foreigners. The answer depends on definitions that Congress
column2: courts
column3: and intelligence agencies have spent two decades arguing about
column4: largely in secret.

column1: Section 702 was added to FISA through the FISA Amendments Act of 2008. It authorizes the Intelligence Community to collect communications of non-US persons located outside the United States for foreign intelligence purposes. The government does not need an individualized warrant to target a specific foreigner abroad. The Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court approves targeting procedures and minimization procedures in bulk
column2: reviewing the program's rules rather than individual targets. That structural feature is what makes Section 702 legally distinct from traditional FISA orders
column3: and legally contested.

column1: The incidental collection problem flows directly from how modern communications work. When the NSA targets a foreign national's email account
column2: it does not collect only messages sent to other foreigners. It collects everything in that account or passing through that infrastructure. American citizens who communicate with targeted foreign nationals have their messages swept into NSA databases without any individualized legal process directed at them. The government calls this ""incidental"" collection. Critics call it a backdoor search regime waiting to happen.

column1: Upstream and Downstream: Two Collection Methods

Section 702 operates through two technically distinct collection programs that the government calls ""upstream"" and ""downstream"" collection. Understanding the difference matters because they implicate different legal concerns and different volumes of American communications.
Downstream collection
column2: historically associated with the PRISM program revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013
column3: involves obtaining communications directly from electronic communications service providers. The NSA sends a directive to companies like Google
column4: Microsoft
column5: or Apple requiring them to hand over communications associated with specific foreign targets. The provider acts as an intermediary. The legal basis is a directive backed by a FISC order. The NSA receives discrete communications tied to identified selectors
column6: such as email addresses or phone numbers associated with targeted foreign nationals.

column1: Upstream collection is more aggressive. The NSA copies internet traffic in bulk as it transits the internet backbone
column2: the fiber optic cables and switching infrastructure that carry American and international communications together. The agency then filters that traffic to extract communications to
column3: from
column4: or about specific targeted selectors. The ""about"" collection feature
column5: which captured communications that merely mentioned a target rather than involving the target directly
column6: generated significant legal controversy and was reportedly discontinued in 2017 after FISC raised compliance concerns. Upstream collection creates a far higher risk of capturing purely domestic communications
column7: because the copying happens at the infrastructure level before any filtering occurs.

column1: The Backdoor Search Problem

The term ""backdoor search"" refers to a specific practice: FBI agents query Section 702 databases using search terms associated with American citizens. Section 702 prohibits the government from using the authority to intentionally target Americans. But it does not prohibit querying the collected database for Americans' communications after the fact. Critics argue this distinction is legally meaningless. The government collected the communications without a warrant
column2: and now agents search them without a warrant
column3: effectively bypassing the Fourth Amendment's warrant requirement through a technical sequencing argument.

column1: The FBI's querying practices became the central controversy in Section 702 oversight debates. The bureau searches 702 databases as a routine part of domestic criminal investigations
column2: not just counterterrorism or counterintelligence work. Agents enter American names
column3: social security numbers
column4: and other identifiers into systems that search communications collected under a foreign intelligence authority. A federal judge reviewing an individualized criminal case receives no automatic notice that evidence derived from 702 collection contributed to the investigation.

column1: CB Factoid: The Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board's ""Report on the Surveillance Program Operated Pursuant to Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act
column2: adopted December 2023
column3: found that the FBI conducted approximately 204
column4: 000 U.S. person queries of Section 702 data in 2022
column5: down from approximately 3.4 million in 2021
column6: following new querying approval requirements implemented in 2021 (Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board
column7: December 2023).

column1: The drop in query numbers between 2021 and 2022 followed an internal FBI policy change requiring agents to obtain approval before running US person queries. Critics noted the new procedure was internal and administrative
column2: not judicially supervised. The underlying legal framework still permitted the searches. The number reduction reflected compliance culture changes
column3: not a legal prohibition.

column1: PCLOB 2023: Compliance Failures Documented

The Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board released its most comprehensive Section 702 assessment in December 2023. The report found systemic compliance problems alongside documented intelligence value. Both conclusions were real
column2: and the government's supporters and critics each emphasized different parts of the same document.

column1: On compliance
column2: the PCLOB found multiple categories of violations. These included improper queries run without the required justification
column3: queries that used prohibited terms
column4: and instances where analysts ran searches for reasons that did not meet the foreign intelligence or national security standards the law requires. The board found the FBI's compliance problems were not isolated incidents but reflected recurring patterns across field offices and case agents with varying levels of training and oversight.

column1: CB Factoid: The Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court's declassified opinion in In re Accuracy Concerns Regarding FBI Matters Submitted to the FISC
column2: issued November 2019 and released publicly in 2020
column3: documented that FBI applications to the court contained errors and unsupported factual claims in a reviewed sample
column4: prompting the court to require additional oversight procedures and raise concerns about institutional accuracy (Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court
column5: November 2019).

column1: The FISC's own compliance opinions
column2: several of which were declassified under court and congressional pressure
column3: documented similar problems. Judges found that the government had misrepresented the scope of its collection
column4: failed to implement required minimization procedures
column5: and continued practices after being directed to stop them. The court's options for enforcement are limited. It can modify or deny future applications
column6: but it has no mechanism to suppress already-collected information or impose sanctions on individual agents.

column1: PCLOB members disagreed on recommendations. A majority supported reauthorization with reforms. A dissenting faction argued the compliance failures were severe enough to warrant either a warrant requirement for US person queries or a fundamental restructuring of how 702 data could be accessed in domestic criminal investigations.
CB Factoid: The Office of the Director of National Intelligence's ""Annual Statistical Transparency Report Regarding the Intelligence Community's Use of National Security Surveillance Authorities
column2: released April 2024
column3: reported that the NSA targeted approximately 181
column4: 599 foreign selectors under Section 702 during calendar year 2023 (Office of the Director of National Intelligence
column5: April 2024).

column1: The 2024 Reauthorization: What Changed and What Did Not
Congress reauthorized Section 702 in April 2024 through the Reforming Intelligence and Securing America Act. The reauthorization extended the program for two years rather than the standard five
column2: reflecting congressional discomfort but not enough consensus to impose structural changes. The debate was one of the most contentious surveillance votes in years
column3: with an unusual coalition of progressive Democrats and libertarian-leaning Republicans pushing for a warrant requirement on US person queries.

column1: The warrant requirement amendment failed by a single vote in the House. That outcome defined the entire reauthorization debate. Supporters of the warrant requirement argued that any system permitting warrantless access to Americans' communications
column2: regardless of how those communications were originally collected
column3: violated the Fourth Amendment's core purpose. Intelligence officials argued that a warrant requirement would make time-sensitive searches operationally impossible and would eliminate a critical tool for identifying domestic threats.

column1: What the reauthorization did include was a modest expansion of the definition of electronic communications service providers
column2: a change that critics argued dramatically expanded the potential reach of 702 collection. The new definition could cover <a href=""https://www.aclu.org/press-releases/congress-passes-fisa-reauthorization-expanding-surveillance-powers"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""ACLU Press Release on FISA Reauthorization"" data-summary=""ACLU statement arguing the 2024 reauthorization expanded the definition of electronic communications service providers to potentially include landlords
column3: hotels
column4: and other businesses with access to communications infrastructure."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">businesses with access to communications infrastructure
column5: potentially including data centers
column6: landlords
column7: and other entities not traditionally understood as telecommunications providers. Congress added technical language to limit the expansion's scope
column8: but civil liberties organizations argued the limiting language was insufficient.

column1: The reauthorization also included new reporting and compliance requirements directing the FBI to track query justifications more rigorously and report compliance failures to PCLOB. Reformers noted that enhanced reporting requirements were welcome but did not address the underlying legal framework that permitted the searches in the first place.
Clapper v. Amnesty International: The Standing Problem
The Supreme Court's 2013 decision in Clapper v. Amnesty International USA effectively closed federal courts to most challenges against Section 702. The case involved journalists
column2: lawyers
column3: and human rights organizations who argued that their work required communicating with people abroad who were likely 702 targets. They claimed the surveillance chilled their professional activities and violated their constitutional rights.

column1: The Court ruled five to four that the plaintiffs lacked standing to sue. Justice Alito's majority opinion held that the harm the plaintiffs alleged was too speculative. They could not prove their communications had actually been collected
column2: because the government kept that information classified. The Court rejected the argument that <a href=""https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/568/398/"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""Clapper v. Amnesty International USA
column3: 568 U.S. 398 (2013)"" data-summary=""Supreme Court ruling holding that plaintiffs challenging Section 702 surveillance lacked Article III standing because they could not demonstrate a sufficiently concrete and imminent injury from the surveillance program."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">reasonable fear of surveillance constituted sufficient injury for Article III standing purposes.

column1: Justice Breyer's dissent argued that the majority's standing doctrine created a structural immunity. The government classifies information about who it surveils. Citizens cannot learn whether they are surveilled. Courts then dismiss challenges because citizens cannot prove surveillance occurred. The result is that a significant domestic surveillance question receives no judicial review on the merits. The dissent called this outcome troubling in a constitutional democracy that relies on judicial oversight of executive power.

The standing problem has not been resolved by subsequent litigation. Criminal defendants notified that evidence derived from 702 collection contributed to their prosecution have raised Fourth Amendment challenges
column2: but courts have largely upheld the program on the merits or found procedural grounds to avoid ruling on it. The combination of a favorable standing doctrine for the government and a sympathetic FISC process has left Section 702 largely insulated from adversarial judicial scrutiny for its entire existence.

column1: The 2024 reauthorization vote and the PCLOB report together capture the political equilibrium around Section 702. There is documented evidence of serious compliance failures. There is bipartisan agreement that some reform is warranted. There is no consensus on what reform would be sufficient
column2: and the two-year reauthorization window means Congress will revisit the question in 2026 with the same unresolved arguments about warrants
column3: standing
column4:
and the meaning of incidental collection still waiting for answers.

"

CB-000221 "Solitary Confinement: What UN Standards Require vs. What US Prisons Actually Do" "solitary-confinement-un-standards-nelson-mandela-rules-us-prison-practice-documented" "law_and_justice" "published" "91" "Solitary Confinement UN Standards vs US Prison | CB-000221" "UN Nelson Mandela Rules 2015; BJS Prisoners 2023; Vera Institute solitary count 2024; Ashker v. Governor of California settlement" "solitary confinement|prison reform|UN Nelson Mandela Rules|incarceration|human rights" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "The UN Nelson Mandela Rules set limits on solitary confinement in 2015. The United States voted for them. The documented US prison practice diverges significantly from those standards." "

The United States holds roughly 5% of the world's population but accounts for a disproportionate share of people held in solitary confinement. That gap between American practice and internationally recognized standards has drawn sustained attention from legal advocates
column2: mental health professionals
column3: and human rights bodies. Understanding that gap requires looking at what the standards actually say
column4: what happens inside isolation units
column5: and how courts and legislatures have responded.

column1: The Nelson Mandela Rules and the 15-Day Limit

column2: the United Nations General Assembly adopted a revised set of minimum standards for the treatment of prisoners
column3: formally titled the <a href=""https://www.unodc.org/documents/justice-and-prison-reform/Nelson_Mandela_Rules-E-ebook.pdf"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""UN Nelson Mandela Rules"" data-summary=""The 2015 UN minimum standards for treatment of prisoners
column4: replacing the 1955 Standard Minimum Rules."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">United Nations Standard Minimum Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners
column5: commonly called the Nelson Mandela Rules. The revised rules replaced an older 1955 framework and introduced specific
column6: enforceable language about solitary confinement that the earlier version lacked.

column1: Rules 43 through 46 form the core of the framework's approach to isolation. Rule 43 prohibits certain practices outright
column2: regardless of the stated justification. It bars indefinite solitary confinement and prolonged solitary confinement
column3: defining prolonged as any period exceeding 15 consecutive days. The rule also prohibits placing people in solitary confinement under conditions that amount to cruel
column4: inhuman
column5: or degrading treatment. Rule 44 defines solitary confinement as the confinement of a prisoner for 22 or more hours per day without meaningful human contact.

column1: CB Factoid: Rule 43 of the Nelson Mandela Rules states: ""In no circumstances may restrictions or disciplinary sanctions amount to torture or other cruel
column2: inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. The following practices
column3: in particular
column4: shall be prohibited: (a) Indefinite solitary confinement; (b) Prolonged solitary confinement; (c) Placement of a prisoner in a dark or constantly lit cell."" (United Nations Standard Minimum Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners
column5: UN General Assembly Resolution 70/175
column6: adopted December 17

column1: Rules 45 and 46 restrict when solitary may be used at all. Rule 45 limits its application to exceptional cases as a last resort
column2: requires review by competent authorities
column3: and prohibits its use for people with mental illness or other conditions that would be aggravated by isolation. Rule 46 requires that a physician examine anyone placed in solitary confinement immediately after placement and then daily
column4: with authority to recommend removal if continued isolation poses health risks.

column1: The United States has not ratified the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights in a way that makes these rules domestically enforceable. The Nelson Mandela Rules function as a benchmark rather than binding law inside American courts. That distinction matters practically
column2: because it means facilities are not legally required to comply even when their practices directly contradict the rules' explicit prohibitions.

column1: The Scale of US Isolation Practices
Measuring solitary confinement in the United States is difficult because no single federal agency collects uniform data across all state systems
column2: local jails
column3: immigration detention facilities
column4: and the federal Bureau of Prisons. The Vera Institute of Justice has produced the most comprehensive estimates available through direct surveys of correctional systems and analysis of administrative data.

column1: CB Factoid: The Vera Institute of Justice's 2024 report ""Solitary Confinement: An Overview"" documented that on any given day approximately 122
column2: 840 people are held in solitary confinement across US state and federal prisons
column3: local jails
column4: and immigration detention facilities
column5: representing roughly 6% of the total incarcerated population (Vera Institute of Justice
column6: ""Solitary Confinement: An Overview
column7: published 2024).

column1: States with the highest documented rates of isolation use include Texas
column2: Louisiana
column3: and Florida. These states combine large prison populations with policies that permit long-term administrative segregation and disciplinary isolation with limited external oversight. Some people in these systems have spent years or decades in conditions meeting the Nelson Mandela Rules' definition of solitary confinement
column4: far exceeding the 15-day threshold the rules treat as the outer boundary of permissible use.

column1: The Vera Institute data also reveals significant variation in how facilities classify and report isolation. Some systems use administrative segregation
column2: special housing units
column3: restrictive housing
column4: or protective custody as distinct categories. People held in these units often experience conditions identical to punitive isolation
column5: but the administrative label affects whether they appear in solitary confinement counts at all.

column1: Psychological Consequences

The American Psychological Association has taken a documented position on the mental health effects of solitary confinement. The APA's Council of Representatives adopted a resolution in 2016 calling for the elimination of solitary confinement for juveniles and people with serious mental illness
column2: and for sharp limits on its use for all incarcerated people. The resolution cited a documented body of research linking isolation to anxiety
column3: depression
column4: hallucinations
column5: cognitive impairment
column6: and increased risk of self-harm and suicide.

column1: Stuart Grassian
column2: a psychiatrist at Harvard Medical School
column3: conducted some of the earliest systematic research on prisoners held in isolation. His studies documented a specific psychiatric syndrome produced by solitary confinement
column4: characterized by hypersensitivity to stimuli
column5: perceptual distortions
column6: paranoia
column7: and difficulties with thinking and concentration. The syndrome appeared in people who had no prior psychiatric history
column8: indicating that isolation itself causes the damage rather than simply worsening pre-existing conditions.

column1: The APA's position documents note that these effects intensify with duration. People held in isolation for weeks develop symptoms that people held for months develop more severely
column2: and people held for years sometimes experience permanent cognitive and psychological damage. The irreversibility of some harm is part of the basis for the Nelson Mandela Rules' 15-day limit
column3: which reflects the point at which research shows severe effects begin to accelerate.

column1: Ashker v. Governor of California

The most significant American legal challenge to indefinite solitary confinement produced a settlement in 2015 that directly mirrors the concerns behind the Nelson Mandela Rules. Ashker v. Governor of California began as a series of individual lawsuits filed by prisoners held in the Security Housing Unit at Pelican Bay State Prison in California. The Pelican Bay SHU held people in single-person cells for 22.5 hours per day
column2: with no congregate activities and severely restricted contact with family members.

column1: Many plaintiffs in the case had been held in those conditions for more than ten years. Some had been there for more than twenty years. Their placement was not time-limited. They remained in the SHU under a gang validation system that kept them there indefinitely based on alleged gang affiliation rather than specific rule violations with fixed sentences. The Center for Constitutional Rights consolidated the individual cases into a class action and argued that the conditions and the indeterminate nature of placement constituted cruel and unusual punishment under the Eighth Amendment.
CB Factoid: The September 1
column3: settlement in Ashker v. Governor of California required California to end indefinite solitary confinement based solely on gang affiliation
column4: transfer class members held more than 10 years in the SHU to general population or step-down programs
column5: and prohibit placement in the SHU without specific findings of serious rule violations with a maximum two-year term for most offenses (Settlement Agreement
column6: Ashker v. Governor of California
column8: United States District Court for the Northern District of California
column9: September 1

column1: The settlement applied to all people held long-term in California's SHU system
column2: not just the named plaintiffs. California eliminated indeterminate SHU sentences based on status alone
column3: created a step-down program for people transitioning out of long-term isolation
column4: and placed limits on total SHU duration for most disciplinary violations. The settlement became a model for advocates in other states arguing that duration and indefiniteness
column5: not isolation itself
column6: constitute the core constitutional and humanitarian problem.

column1: ADX Florence and Federal Placement
The federal Bureau of Prisons operates the Administrative Maximum facility in Florence
column2: Colorado
column3: known as ADX Florence. It is the most restrictive facility in the federal system and holds people in conditions meeting the definition of solitary confinement under the Nelson Mandela Rules. Cells measure approximately 7 by 12 feet. Prisoners spend 22 to 23 hours per day inside them. Meals come through a slot in the door.

column1: Placement at ADX requires a referral from another federal facility based on documented behavior. The Bureau of Prisons policy requires findings that the person presents a serious threat to institutional security that cannot be managed through other placement. A 2012 lawsuit
column2: Cunningham v. Federal Bureau of Prisons
column3: challenged ADX placement procedures and eventually produced revisions to the administrative review process. The revisions did not eliminate solitary conditions but added procedural protections around placement decisions.

column1: ADX Florence currently holds approximately 300 to 400 people at any given time. Critics note that some prisoners have spent decades there. The facility's conditions exceed the Nelson Mandela Rules' 15-day limit by orders of magnitude for most of its population
column2: and the indeterminate nature of many placements directly conflicts with Rule 43's prohibition on indefinite isolation.

column1: State Reforms
Several states have moved toward statutory limits on isolation in the years since the Ashker settlement. Colorado enacted legislation in 2017 and updated it in subsequent years
column2: placing a 15-day limit on consecutive isolation for most prisoners and banning solitary confinement for pregnant people
column3: juveniles
column4: and people with serious mental illness. The 15-day figure directly tracks the Nelson Mandela Rules threshold.

column1: New York enacted the HALT Solitary Confinement Act in 2021 after years of legislative effort. The law limits isolation to 15 consecutive days and no more than 20 days in any 60-day period. It requires that people with mental illness
column2: serious medical conditions
column3: developmental disabilities
column4: and other vulnerabilities be diverted from solitary entirely. New York's law is among the most comprehensive in the country and explicitly aligns its durational limits with the Nelson Mandela Rules framework.

column1: Massachusetts enacted solitary confinement reforms through a broader criminal justice reform bill
column2: setting limits on isolation duration and requiring heightened review for extended placements. Several other states
column3: including Maine and Connecticut
column4: have passed legislation restricting solitary use for juveniles and people with mental illness
column5: moving incrementally toward the Nelson Mandela Rules standard without adopting it wholesale.

column1: The gap between international standards and US practice remains large. The Nelson Mandela Rules set a 15-day ceiling. Most American systems have no statutory ceiling at all. Where reform has happened
column2: it has come through litigation producing settlements
column3: legislative campaigns producing statutes
column4:
or both working together over extended periods. The Ashker settlement demonstrated that indefiniteness is a legally vulnerable feature of isolation regimes. State reform laws have demonstrated that durational limits are politically achievable. The distance between those incremental gains and full compliance with international standards measures the work that remains.

"

column1:
CB-000222 "Algorithmic Hiring Discrimination: EEOC Guidance and the Cases That Followed" "algorithmic-hiring-discrimination-eeoc-ai-tools-documented-cases-2023-2024" "technology" "published" "91" "Algorithmic Hiring Discrimination EEOC Guidance AI | CB-000222" "EEOC May 2023 Technical Assistance on AI; EEOC v. iTutorGroup (2023) settlement; Chicago Employment Decision Tools Ordinance 2022" "AI hiring|algorithmic discrimination|EEOC|Title VII|employment" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "Employers increasingly use AI-powered tools to screen job applicants. The EEOC issued guidance on when those tools violate Title VII. The enforcement record is beginning to build." "

Automated hiring tools now screen millions of job applications before any human reviewer reads a single line of text. Companies use these systems to rank resumes
column2: analyze video interviews
column3: schedule candidate calls
column4: and flag applicants for rejection or advancement. The technology promises efficiency and consistency. It also carries documented risks of encoding discrimination into hiring pipelines at industrial scale.

column1: Understanding how these tools function requires looking at each stage of the hiring funnel where automation has taken hold. Resume screening software parses uploaded documents and scores candidates against weighted criteria. Those criteria might include keyword matching
column2: employment gap detection
column3: educational credentials
column4: or inferred proximity to prior successful hires. Video interview platforms record candidates answering preset questions
column5: then run the recordings through computer vision and natural language processing models that score vocal tone
column6: facial movement
column7: word choice
column8: and response structure. Automated scheduling tools filter candidates through availability windows and priority queues before routing them to human interviewers. At each stage
column9: the algorithm makes decisions that humans later treat as objective.

column1: The problem surfaces when those decisions fall harder on members of protected classes. A resume screener trained on historical hiring data may learn to penalize credentials from historically Black colleges and universities if the company's prior workforce lacked diversity. A video analysis tool may score certain speech patterns
column2: accents
column3: or facial expressions as less favorable
column4: affecting candidates by race
column5: national origin
column6: or disability status. An automated scheduling system may screen out candidates who cannot interview during certain hours
column7: which can disparate impact workers with caregiving responsibilities protected under sex discrimination law.

column1: CB Factoid: The EEOC's May 2023 technical assistance document
column2: ""Artificial Intelligence and Algorithmic Fairness
column3: stated that employers remain liable under Title VII
column4: the ADA
column5: and the ADEA when they use AI hiring tools that produce adverse impact against protected classes
column6: even if the employer did not design the tool
column7: citing the EEOC's longstanding position that covered entities cannot delegate their legal obligations to third-party vendors (EEOC Technical Assistance Document
column8: ""Artificial Intelligence and Algorithmic Fairness
column9: May 18
column11: U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission).

column1: The EEOC issued that guidance in May 2023 as part of its broader initiative on AI and algorithmic fairness. The document clarified that existing civil rights statutes apply fully to algorithmic tools. If an AI system produces a selection rate for a protected class that falls below the legally recognized threshold for adverse impact
column2: the employer faces the same liability it would face using a discriminatory paper application or a biased human screener. The guidance further specified that employers cannot escape liability by pointing to a vendor's representations about bias testing. Employers must independently verify that tools they deploy comply with applicable law.

column1: The practical stakes became concrete in August 2023 when the EEOC announced a settlement in EEOC v. iTutorGroup. That case marked the agency's first enforcement action specifically targeting an AI-driven hiring system. iTutorGroup
column2: an online education company
column3: used an automated application screening tool that the EEOC alleged systematically rejected applications from women over 55 and men over 60. The system reportedly flagged age-related information during the screening process and used it to filter out older applicants before any human reviewer evaluated their qualifications.

column1: CB Factoid: The EEOC reached a $365
column2: 000 settlement with iTutorGroup in August 2023
column3: requiring the company to implement an anti-discrimination policy
column4: provide training to personnel involved in hiring
column5: and report hiring data to the EEOC for a two-year monitoring period
column6: marking the first EEOC enforcement action resolved specifically addressing AI-driven hiring discrimination under the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (EEOC Press Release
column7: ""EEOC Settles First AI Hiring Discrimination Case
column8: August 9
column10: U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission).

column1: The iTutorGroup settlement required payment of $365
column2: 000 to affected applicants. The company also agreed to adopt a written anti-discrimination policy
column3: provide training to staff involved in hiring decisions
column4: and submit hiring data to the EEOC for two years so the agency could monitor compliance. The case sent a clear signal that the EEOC intended to treat algorithmic discrimination as actionable under existing statutes without waiting for new legislation. It also confirmed that automated rejection at the screening stage
column5: before any human contact with an applicant
column6: qualifies as an employment practice subject to civil rights law.

column1: Assessing whether an AI hiring tool produces unlawful discrimination requires a method for measuring disparate impact. Federal employment law has used the four-fifths rule
column2: also called the 80 percent rule
column3: for decades. Under this standard
column4: a selection procedure produces adverse impact if the selection rate for a protected group is less than four-fifths of the selection rate for the group with the highest selection rate. If a hiring tool advances 50 percent of white applicants and only 30 percent of Black applicants
column5: the ratio is 60 percent
column6: which falls below the 80 percent threshold and indicates adverse impact requiring justification.

column1: Applying this rule to algorithmic tools raises measurement challenges. Many employers do not collect or retain data on how AI screening affects protected classes at each decision point. The four-fifths calculation requires knowing selection rates by demographic group
column2: but automated systems often process thousands of candidates without generating the granular outputs necessary for that analysis. The EEOC's Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures
column3: originally issued in 1978
column4: require employers to maintain records sufficient to determine whether selection procedures produce adverse impact. Those recordkeeping obligations apply to algorithmic tools the same way they apply to written tests or structured interviews.

column1: Local governments began moving faster than federal regulators on mandatory auditing requirements. Chicago enacted the Employment Decision Tools Ordinance in 2022
column2: becoming the first city in the United States to require employers to conduct bias audits before deploying automated employment decision tools.

column1: CB Factoid: Chicago's Employment Decision Tools Ordinance
column2: passed in 2022
column3: requires employers with at least 15 employees in Chicago to conduct an independent bias audit of any automated employment decision tool before using it in hiring
column4: and to provide notice to applicants and employees that such tools will be used
column5: with audits updated at least annually and made publicly available (Chicago Employment Decision Tools Ordinance
column6: Municipal Code of Chicago Section 2-160-100
column7: enacted 2022
column8: City of Chicago).

column1: The Chicago ordinance applies to employers with at least 15 employees in the city. It requires an independent bias audit before deployment of any covered tool
column2: with audits repeated at least annually and made publicly available. Employers must also notify applicants and employees that automated tools will be used in their evaluation. The ordinance builds on New York City's Local Law 144
column3: which took effect in 2023 and similarly requires bias audits of automated employment decision tools used by employers in New York City. Together these local laws represent the emerging regulatory layer below federal civil rights statutes
column4: creating affirmative compliance obligations rather than waiting for enforcement actions after harm occurs.

column1: Independent researchers have documented specific failures in widely used systems. A Princeton University audit of HireVue's video interview analysis product found that the system's scoring varied with factors that correlate with protected class status but have no documented relationship to job performance. Researchers testing the platform found that video quality
column2: background environments
column3: and speech patterns associated with regional accents affected scores in ways that could systematically disadvantage certain demographic groups. HireVue subsequently discontinued its facial action unit analysis component in 2021 following public scrutiny
column4: though the company maintained that its remaining analytical features do not encode bias.

column1: Stanford researchers published findings on facial analysis software bias showing that commercial systems performed significantly worse on darker-skinned individuals and on women compared to lighter-skinned men. These findings extended earlier work by researchers at MIT
column2: which demonstrated error rates for commercial facial recognition software that differed substantially by race and gender. When video interview platforms use facial analysis scoring as part of candidate evaluation
column3: those documented performance gaps translate directly into disparate outcomes for applicants.

column1: Employers using these tools face layered legal risk. A vendor's contractual warranty that a product has been tested for bias does not transfer liability away from the employer under federal civil rights law. The EEOC's May 2023 guidance made that explicit. If an audit reveals adverse impact and the employer cannot demonstrate that the tool is job-related and consistent with business necessity
column2: the employer remains liable for discriminatory outcomes regardless of what the vendor represented during the sales process. Employers also face risk from plaintiffs' attorneys who have begun filing putative class actions based on algorithmic screening
column3: following the template established by the iTutorGroup enforcement action.

column1: The practical compliance path for employers using AI hiring tools involves several documented steps. First
column2: employers should request from vendors the methodology and results of any bias audits conducted on the tool
column3: asking specifically whether those audits analyzed outcomes by race
column4: sex
column5: national origin
column6: disability status
column7: and age. Second
column8: employers should implement their own ongoing monitoring
column9: collecting application and selection data in a format that permits four-fifths calculations at each screening stage. Third
column10: employers operating in Chicago or New York City must satisfy local audit and disclosure requirements independent of whatever testing the vendor has performed. Fourth
column11: employers should review contractual indemnification provisions with vendors
column12: understanding that such provisions provide no defense against government enforcement but may affect cost allocation in private litigation.

column1: Algorithmic hiring tools are not inherently discriminatory. Some automated systems have been developed with rigorous fairness testing and ongoing monitoring that produces more consistent and less biased outcomes than unstructured human review. The legal framework does not prohibit these tools. It requires that employers treat their use the same way civil rights law has always treated any employment selection procedure: measure the outcomes
column2: justify the methodology
column3:
and correct disparate impact when it appears.

"

column1:
CB-000223 "Stingray Devices: Law Enforcement Uses Cell-Site Simulators Without Warrants and the Documented Record" "stingray-cell-site-simulators-law-enforcement-warrantless-use-documented" "technology" "published" "91" "Stingray Cell Site Simulators Warrantless Use | CB-000223" "ACLU Stingray tracker public records; Harris Corporation NDAs; ACLU v. Baltimore Police (2016); DOJ policy on cell-site simulators 2015" "Stingray|cell site simulators|surveillance|Fourth Amendment|law enforcement" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "Cell-site simulators called Stingrays mimic cell towers and capture phone data from everyone nearby. Law enforcement agencies have used them without warrants in documented cases." "

Stingray Surveillance: How Law Enforcement Tracks Phones and What Courts Have Said About It
Cell-site simulators
column2: widely known as Stingrays after a flagship product made by Harris Corporation
column3: have become standard tools in law enforcement arsenals across the United States. Their operation is straightforward in concept and deeply invasive in practice. A Stingray device broadcasts a signal that mimics a legitimate cell tower. Nearby mobile phones automatically connect to the strongest available signal
column4: making them connect to the Stingray instead of the carrier network. Once connected
column5: the device captures each phone's International Mobile Subscriber Identity (IMSI) and International Mobile Equipment Identity (IMEI) numbers
column6: which uniquely identify both the SIM card and the physical handset.

column1: The device does not target one phone at a time. Every phone within range connects to it. Law enforcement then filters the captured data to locate the phone they want. More sophisticated versions of the technology can intercept calls and text messages in real time
column2: and some models can force target devices onto 2G networks
column3: which use weaker encryption that is easier to crack. The surrounding phones
column4: belonging to people with no connection to any investigation
column5: get swept into the dragnet as a matter of course.

column1: CB Factoid: The ACLU's cell-site simulator tracking project
column2: documented in ""Stingray Tracking Devices: Who's Got Them?"" (American Civil Liberties Union
column3: updated through 2023)
column4: identified at least 75 federal agencies and more than 100 local and state law enforcement entities that have purchased cell-site simulator technology using public records requests and budget documents.

column1: The scale of Stingray deployment across American law enforcement remained largely hidden for years. A significant reason for that secrecy traces directly to Harris Corporation
column2: the Florida-based defense contractor that manufactured the devices. Harris required law enforcement agencies to sign nondisclosure agreements before they could purchase or use the equipment. These agreements were not routine vendor contracts. They instructed agencies to withhold information about the technology even in criminal proceedings
column3: even when defendants had a constitutional right to understand what evidence was used against them.

column1: The ACLU obtained copies of these agreements through Freedom of Information Act requests filed with multiple agencies. The documents showed that the FBI
column2: which coordinated Harris's sales to local departments
column3: reviewed and approved the NDAs. In some documented cases
column4: prosecutors dropped charges against defendants rather than reveal how location data had been obtained. The Sarasota Police Department in Florida
column5: for example
column6: declined to disclose Stingray use in a criminal case and had used the device for eight months before receiving any training on its operation
column7: according to ACLU analysis of public records.

column1: Courts have grappled with what these nondisclosure practices mean for defendants. In several documented cases
column2: convictions were reversed or charges dismissed when judges learned that law enforcement had used Stingrays without warrants and had concealed that fact from both prosecutors and courts. A Maryland Court of Special Appeals ruled in 2016 in Blevins v. State that a Stingray had been used without a warrant and that the evidence derived from it was inadmissible under the Fourth Amendment. The court found that police had misrepresented to a judge the nature of the technology they were using
column3: describing it only as a ""pen register"" device
column4: which has a much lower legal threshold for approval.

column1: The Blevins ruling was not isolated. In 2014
column2: a Florida judge suppressed evidence in a robbery case after determining that Tallahassee police had used a Stingray without a warrant. Judge Catherine Brunson wrote that the warrantless use constituted an unreasonable search under the Fourth Amendment. The case was documented by the ACLU
column3: which noted that the Tallahassee Police Department had used Stingrays more than 200 times without ever obtaining a single warrant for their operation.

column1: Federal policy eventually moved to address the warrant gap. In September 2015
column2: the Department of Justice announced a new policy requiring federal law enforcement agencies to obtain a warrant based on probable cause before deploying cell-site simulators in most circumstances.

column1: CB Factoid: The DOJ's ""Policy Guidance: Use of Cell-Site Simulator Technology
column2: issued September 3
column4: by Deputy Attorney General Sally Yates
column5: required Department of Justice law enforcement agencies to obtain a probable-cause warrant before using cell-site simulators and mandated that data collected from non-target phones be deleted promptly (U.S. Department of Justice
column6: September 2015).

column1: The 2015 DOJ policy included several significant requirements. Agencies had to delete data collected from non-target phones promptly after identifying the target device. They could not use Stingray data to build profiles or conduct unrelated investigations. The policy also addressed transparency
column2: requiring that prosecutors disclose Stingray use to defendants in relevant cases.

column1: The policy contained notable exceptions
column2: however. It did not apply to state and local agencies
column3: which conduct the majority of Stingray deployments in the United States. It carved out broad exemptions for exigent circumstances
column4: national security investigations
column5: and cases involving the imminent threat to life or public safety. Critics noted that these exceptions
column6: particularly the national security carve-out
column7: swallowed much of the rule in practice. The policy also did not address the broader constitutional question of whether historical cell-site data or real-time tracking data required a warrant under the Fourth Amendment. That question reached the Supreme Court three years later.

column1: Carpenter v. United States
column2: decided in 2018
column3: began as a straightforward robbery prosecution. The FBI obtained cell-site location information from wireless carriers covering 127 days of Timothy Carpenter's movements. Prosecutors used this data to place him near the scene of several robberies. The government argued it needed no warrant because the records belonged to the phone companies
column4: not to Carpenter
column5: invoking the third-party doctrine established in earlier Supreme Court precedents.

column1: CB Factoid: In Carpenter v. United States
column3: the Supreme Court held 5-4 that accessing seven or more days of historical cell-site location information constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant
column4: with Chief Justice John Roberts writing for the majority that the third-party doctrine does not apply to comprehensive digital location records.

column1: The Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that accessing seven or more days of historical cell-site location records requires a warrant under the Fourth Amendment. Chief Justice John Roberts
column2: writing for the majority
column3: drew a distinction between the limited transactional records at issue in earlier third-party doctrine cases and the comprehensive
column4: near-perfect surveillance record created by modern cell-site data. Roberts wrote that such records provided ""near perfect surveillance"" of a person's movements
column5: something the Founders could not have anticipated and the Fourth Amendment was designed to prevent.

column1: Legal scholars and practitioners quickly examined how Carpenter applied to Stingray technology. The ACLU argued that Carpenter's reasoning applies directly to Stingray deployments
column2: which capture real-time location data in even greater granular detail than carrier records. Several lower courts have agreed. In 2019
column3: a federal district court in the Northern District of Illinois suppressed Stingray-derived location evidence in a gun possession case
column4: applying Carpenter's logic to real-time tracking. The court found that prolonged real-time surveillance of a person's location through a cell-site simulator constituted a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant.

column1: State legislatures have moved faster than Congress to establish statutory warrant requirements for Stingray use. As of 2023
column2: more than a dozen states have enacted laws explicitly requiring law enforcement agencies to obtain warrants before deploying cell-site simulators. California
column3: Washington
column4: Virginia
column5: and Illinois are among the states with such requirements on the books. Several state laws also require annual reporting on how frequently the technology is used and mandate destruction of data collected from non-target phones.

column1: Documented noncompliance with these state laws has occurred even after passage. A 2019 investigative report by the Baltimore Sun found that the Baltimore Police Department had used Stingrays more than 4
column2: 300 times between 2007 and 2015
column3: with minimal judicial oversight. Baltimore's use came under particular scrutiny after a detective testified in a 2015 hearing that department practice was to notify prosecutors that evidence came from ""a confidential source"" rather than disclosing Stingray use
column4: a practice the Baltimore State's Attorney's Office said it was unaware of. The department later acknowledged the practice and said it had changed its disclosure policies.

column1: The transparency problem remains structural. Because many agencies obtained their devices through federal homeland security grants or through FBI arrangements that came with Harris NDAs attached
column2: local oversight mechanisms were bypassed at the point of acquisition. City councils and county commissions that control police budgets often did not know their departments had the technology at all. Some jurisdictions learned about Stingray ownership only after journalists or advocacy groups filed public records requests and obtained purchase orders.

column1: The legal landscape has shifted meaningfully since 2015. Federal agencies now operate under a written warrant requirement. The Supreme Court has extended Fourth Amendment protection to comprehensive cell-site location data. More than a dozen states have codified warrant requirements in statute. Courts have reversed convictions and suppressed evidence in cases where these rules were violated.
What has not changed is the gap between written policy and operational practice. Cell-site simulator technology continues to be deployed by hundreds of agencies
column2: many of which operate without the transparency measures that courts and legislatures have increasingly required. The devices remain effective precisely because the phones of everyone nearby connect to them without warning or consent
column3:
generating data about people who are never charged with any offense and who have no way of knowing the encounter happened. The constitutional and statutory frameworks governing that encounter are now more developed than they were a decade ago. Whether they are consistently followed remains an open and documented question.

"

Every time a car passes a camera mounted on a utility pole
column6: a bridge overpass
column7: or the roof of a patrol vehicle
