column9: converts the image to text
column10: timestamps the record
column11: logs the GPS coordinates
column12: and uploads the package to a cloud database within seconds. That process happens millions of times per day across the United States. The resulting databases contain detailed
column13: time-stamped location histories for ordinary drivers who have never been suspected of any crime.

column1: Automated license plate readers
column2: called ALPRs or LPRs
column3: use optical character recognition software combined with infrared illumination to capture plate data in nearly any lighting condition. Stationary readers mount permanently on poles
column4: gantries
column5: and building facades. Mobile units attach to patrol car rooftops and can sweep entire parking lots or neighborhood streets during routine patrol shifts. Both types feed data upward to centralized cloud platforms operated by private vendors. Individual officers query those platforms through smartphone apps or in-car terminals. The data captured includes not just the plate number but the vehicle's make
column6: color
column7: and any visible identifying features the camera's software flags automatically.

column1: The scale of collection is difficult to overstate. A single mobile reader mounted on a patrol car can capture thousands of plates during one shift. A network of stationary readers installed along major corridors in a mid-sized city can document the movements of virtually every driver in the region over time. Aggregated across hundreds of participating agencies
column2: these systems build comprehensive location profiles without any individualized suspicion
column3: warrant
column4: or even awareness on the part of the person being tracked.

column1: CB Factoid: The ACLU's 2013 report ""You Are Being Tracked: How License Plate Readers Are Being Used to Record Americans' Movements"" documented that police departments in some jurisdictions retained plate records for years and that 85 percent of plate scans captured vehicles of people with no criminal record (American Civil Liberties Union
column2: ""You Are Being Tracked
column3: July 2013).

column1: Flock Safety
column2: an Atlanta-based company founded in 2017
column3: now dominates the government ALPR market in the United States. The company markets its cameras to homeowners associations
column4: private communities
column5: and local governments simultaneously
column6: creating overlapping private and public surveillance networks that feed into shared databases. Flock's platform sells on a subscription model
column7: which means municipalities receive cameras
column8: installation
column9: cloud storage
column10: and software for an annual fee rather than purchasing hardware outright. That structure keeps initial procurement costs low and makes the service appear in government budgets as a recurring service contract rather than a capital expenditure.

column1: Public records requests filed by journalists and advocacy organizations have surfaced thousands of contracts between Flock Safety and local law enforcement agencies across dozens of states. Those contracts often include provisions enabling participating agencies to share data across the platform's entire network. An agency in one county can query plate sightings logged by cameras operated by agencies in different states
column2: all through a single interface. Flock calls this capability its ""nationwide"" network. The practical result is that a small-town police department with a modest camera deployment gains access to location data captured by hundreds of other agencies without any additional legal process.

column1: CB Factoid: Investigative reporting and public records analysis by Wired and other outlets documented that Flock Safety held contracts with more than 4
column2: 000 law enforcement agencies across the United States as of 2023
column3: with inter-agency data sharing enabled by default across the platform (Wired
column4: ""Flock Safety Wants to Put Its Cameras Everywhere
column5: April 2023).

column1: The inter-agency sharing architecture raises a question that individual agency contracts do not answer: who governs access to the aggregated dataset? Flock's terms of service and individual municipal contracts generally define permissible uses at a high level of abstraction
column2: referring to ""law enforcement purposes"" without specifying what that phrase covers. When an agency in one jurisdiction shares its camera feed with agencies elsewhere through the platform
column3: the originating agency's local policies may not apply. Oversight gaps open in the spaces between jurisdictions.

column1: Retention practices compound those gaps. Many agencies store plate data for one
column2: two
column3: or three years. Some retain records indefinitely. Because most ALPR data captures the movements of people with no connection to any crime
column4: long retention periods mean that the location history of an average driver accumulates continuously in a database she has no legal right to inspect
column5: correct
column6: or delete. A record showing that a vehicle passed a particular camera on a particular date becomes increasingly sensitive over time as more records accumulate around it. A year of plate sightings can reveal where someone works
column7: worships
column8: seeks medical care
column9: sleeps
column10: and who they associate with.

column1: The documented harms from ALPR data misuse have moved from theoretical to concrete. Journalists and civil liberties researchers have documented law enforcement officers using ALPR queries to track former romantic partners
column2: ex-spouses
column3: and individuals they knew personally. Because access logs for ALPR queries often lack rigorous auditing
column4: unauthorized searches can go undetected for extended periods. The Electronic Frontier Foundation and other organizations have catalogued cases in which officers faced discipline or termination for using ALPR systems to conduct personal surveillance rather than legitimate investigations.

column1: Immigration enforcement agencies have integrated ALPR data into their operations extensively. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement has accessed commercial ALPR databases
column2: including those maintained by Vigilant Solutions
column3: a private aggregator that sells access to its repository of billions of plate records to federal and local agencies. Government contracts and procurement records obtained through Freedom of Information Act requests confirm that ICE paid for access to Vigilant's LPR data. That access enables immigration agents to build location histories for individuals without obtaining judicial authorization. The data can show when a vehicle crossed into a particular state
column4: appeared near a border checkpoint
column5: or visited locations associated with immigration services or legal aid organizations.

column1: The constitutional framework governing this surveillance is unsettled. In
column2: Supreme Court Opinion"" data-summary=""Supreme Court majority opinion holding that obtaining historical cell-site location information constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Carpenter v. United States
column3: decided in 2018
column4: the Supreme Court held that the government's warrantless collection of seven days of historical cell-site location information from a wireless carrier constituted a search under the Fourth Amendment. Chief Justice Roberts's majority opinion emphasized that the retrospective quality of the data
column5: its comprehensive nature
column6: and the fact that it was collected without any active participation by the subject distinguished it from the ordinary third-party doctrine cases the Court had decided in earlier decades.

column1: ALPR databases map directly onto the concerns the Carpenter majority articulated. Location data logged by a network of fixed and mobile readers produces exactly the kind of ""detailed
column2: encyclopedic
column3: effortlessly compiled"" record that Roberts described as qualitatively different from the limited transaction records at issue in older cases. Advocates and legal scholars argue that Carpenter's logic extends to ALPR data
column4: particularly when that data spans weeks or months of an individual's movements. Courts have not yet resolved that question conclusively
column5: and the result is a legal environment where agencies continue building and querying these databases while Fourth Amendment protection remains uncertain.

column1: The third-party doctrine presents the central obstacle. Plate numbers are visible to anyone in public
column2: and every time a driver operates a vehicle on a public road
column3: she arguably exposes that information voluntarily. Agencies and vendors rely on this reasoning to argue that ALPR collection requires no warrant. Carpenter weakened but did not eliminate the third-party doctrine
column4: and lower courts have reached different conclusions about whether its reasoning applies to ALPR data specifically. The legal landscape will not stabilize until the Supreme Court addresses ALPR surveillance directly or Congress imposes a statutory framework.

column1: Several states have moved ahead with statutory restrictions. New Hampshire enacted legislation prohibiting law enforcement agencies from retaining plate data that does not match a hot list
column2: meaning data captured on vehicles associated with no active investigation
column3: warrant
column4: or alert. Under that framework
column5: non-hit records must be deleted almost immediately rather than stored in a searchable historical database. The practical effect is to limit ALPR systems to their stated purpose of identifying wanted vehicles rather than building location archives on the general population.

column1: CB Factoid: New Hampshire Revised Statutes Annotated Section 261:75-c prohibits law enforcement agencies from retaining automated license plate recognition data on vehicles that do not match an active alert or investigative target
column2: requiring deletion of non-hit data and restricting use to specific law enforcement purposes (New Hampshire RSA 261:75-c
column3: enacted 2019).

column1: Other states have imposed retention caps rather than deletion requirements. Maine limits retention to 21 days for non-hit data. Vermont requires data destruction within 18 months. Arkansas
column2: Montana
column3: and Utah have enacted statutes requiring either short retention windows or prohibiting the sale or sharing of plate data with other jurisdictions. These approaches vary in strength and enforcement mechanisms
column4: and gaps remain in most state frameworks. No federal statute currently governs ALPR retention
column5: sharing
column6: or access.

column1: The contrast between restrictive states and permissive ones creates a patchwork that sophisticated actors can exploit. An agency in a state with no retention limits can query data on behalf of partner agencies from states with strict rules
column2: effectively routing around local restrictions through a shared platform. Flock's nationwide network makes this kind of regulatory arbitrage straightforward. A query entered by an officer in Georgia can surface plate sightings logged by cameras in New Hampshire
column3: regardless of what New Hampshire's statutes say about how long that data may be retained at the local level.

column1: The policy options under discussion include mandatory data minimization requirements
column2: prohibition on inter-agency sharing without judicial authorization
column3: audit log requirements that create accountable records of every query
column4: and private rights of action allowing individuals to sue agencies or vendors for unauthorized data access. None of these proposals has advanced at the federal level. Meanwhile
column5: camera networks continue to expand
column6: retention periods continue accumulating
column7:
and the location histories of millions of drivers grow longer every day.

Several authors' groups filed related suits. The copyright theory these cases are testing has never been litigated at scale." "

Who Owns the Words That Taught the Machines? AI Copyright Litigation Reaches a Boiling Point
Every large language model starts with text. Enormous quantities of it. Engineers at AI companies have spent years pulling books
column2: articles
column3: forum posts
column4: academic papers
column5: and news archives into training pipelines
column6: processing that material to teach models statistical relationships between words. The resulting systems can draft legal briefs
column7: summarize medical research
column8: and generate marketing copy. They can also
column9: under certain prompting conditions
column10: reproduce the source material nearly word for word. That last capability is now at the center of one of the most consequential intellectual property battles in American legal history.

column1: The core dispute is straightforward to state and genuinely difficult to resolve. Copyright law grants authors and publishers exclusive rights to reproduce
column2: distribute
column3: and create derivative works from their content. AI developers argue their training process constitutes transformative use
column4: not infringement. Rights holders argue that ingesting millions of copyrighted works without payment or permission is theft at industrial scale
column5: regardless of what the output looks like. Courts across the country are now being asked to decide which view the law actually supports.

column1: How Training Data Works
Building a competitive LLM requires training datasets measured in the hundreds of billions to trillions of tokens. OpenAI's GPT series
column2: Meta's LLaMA models
column3: and Google's Gemini systems all drew heavily from Common Crawl
column4: a nonprofit web archive that snapshots publicly accessible internet content without regard for the copyright status of individual pages. Supplementing that foundation
column5: companies used digitized book collections
column6: news archives
column7: academic repositories
column8: and code repositories. None of the major foundation model developers obtained blanket licenses from the authors or publishers whose work filled those datasets. The implicit argument
column9: for years
column10: was that web scraping sat in a legal gray zone similar to search engine indexing
column11: and that training a model on text differed fundamentally from copying it in the traditional sense.

column1: That argument is now being tested in federal courts
column2: with plaintiffs arguing the analogy fails because search engines cache content temporarily and direct users to source pages
column3: while AI companies extracted text permanently
column4: used it to build commercial products
column5: and in some cases compete directly with the original publishers.

column1: New York Times v. Microsoft and OpenAI
CB Factoid: The New York Times Company filed its complaint against Microsoft Corporation and OpenAI Inc. in the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York on December 27
column3: alleging direct copyright infringement
column4: contributory infringement
column5: and trademark dilution (""The New York Times Company v. Microsoft Corporation et al.
column6: filed December 27
column8: U.S.D.C. S.D.N.Y.).

column1: The
column2: alleging copyright infringement and other claims."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Times complaint runs to 69 pages and includes an exhibit that proved immediately striking to legal observers. The newspaper documented dozens of instances in which ChatGPT
column3: when prompted appropriately
column4: reproduced Times articles at length and with high fidelity. The exhibit shows side-by-side comparisons: original Times journalism on one side
column5: ChatGPT output on the other. The reproduced passages include investigative reporting
column6: feature articles
column7: and news coverage
column8: sometimes running hundreds of words without meaningful variation from the original text.

column1: CB Factoid: Exhibit J of the NYT complaint contains side-by-side comparisons showing ChatGPT reproducing Times articles nearly verbatim
column2: including a passage from a 2022 Wirecutter review of air mattresses that matched the original text for several paragraphs with minimal deviation (""The New York Times Company v. Microsoft Corporation et al.
column3: Exhibit J
column4: filed December 27
column6: U.S.D.C. S.D.N.Y.).

column1: The Times makes several distinct legal claims. First
column2: it alleges Microsoft and OpenAI copied millions of its articles without authorization to train GPT models. Second
column3: it argues the resulting models threaten the Times's core business by allowing users to retrieve news content without visiting the Times website or paying for a subscription. Third
column4: it raises claims related to the Bing search integration
column5: where Microsoft's AI-powered search feature surfaces Times content in ways the paper contends substitute for direct visits. The complaint explicitly frames this as an existential economic threat
column6: noting that the Times invested heavily in original journalism that OpenAI captured for free and now monetizes through commercial API access and Microsoft's Copilot products.

column1: OpenAI's public responses have emphasized that the memorization examples required specific adversarial prompting unlikely to occur in normal use
column2: and that the company has worked to reduce such outputs. The legal sufficiency of that response remains to be tested. The case was still in early litigation stages as of mid-2025
column3: with defendants seeking to narrow or dismiss certain claims.

column1: The Fair Use Defense
OpenAI's primary legal shield is the fair use doctrine codified at
column2: listing four factors courts consider when evaluating whether a use is fair."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">17 U.S.C. § 107. The statute requires courts to weigh four factors: the purpose and character of the use
column3: the nature of the copyrighted work
column4: the amount and substantiality of the portion used
column5: and the effect of the use on the potential market for the original.

column1: CB Factoid: 17 U.S.C. § 107
column2: enacted as part of the Copyright Act of 1976
column3: identifies four nonexclusive factors for fair use analysis: (1) the purpose and character of the use
column4: including whether it is commercial or nonprofit educational; (2) the nature of the copyrighted work; (3) the amount and substantiality of the portion used; and (4) the effect of the use upon the potential market for the work (Copyright Act of 1976
column5: Public Law 94-553
column6: enacted October 19
column8: United States Congress).

column1: OpenAI argues most forcefully on the first factor
column2: contending that training is transformative because it converts expressive text into statistical weights
column3: producing a fundamentally different kind of output than the original works. The company draws on the Supreme Court's Campbell v. Acuff-Rose (1994) precedent
column4: which established that transformation is the central question in fair use analysis. OpenAI also points to Authors Guild v. Google (2d Cir. 2015)
column5: where the Second Circuit approved Google's book scanning project as transformative because it created a searchable index rather than reproducing readable text.

column1: Plaintiffs contest all four factors. On transformation
column2: they argue that ingesting entire articles to build a commercial product is not meaningfully different from photocopying a library. On the fourth factor
column3: the market harm question
column4: they argue that AI companies are actively building products that compete with the original publishers for advertising dollars
column5: subscription revenue
column6: and information services. The memorization evidence complicates the transformation argument considerably
column7: since verbatim reproduction is difficult to characterize as transformed use. Legal scholars are divided on how courts will ultimately rule
column8: and no circuit court has yet addressed AI training data directly.

column1: Andersen v. Stability AI and the Visual Art Cases
The visual art litigation developed along a parallel track. In January 2023
column2: artists Sarah Andersen
column3: Kelly McKernan
column4: and Karla Ortiz filed suit against <a href=""https://stability.ai/"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""Stability AI"" data-summary=""The company behind the Stable Diffusion image generation model
column5: named as a defendant in multiple AI copyright cases."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Stability AI
column6: Midjourney
column7: and DeviantArt
column8: alleging that image generation models trained on billions of scraped images infringed their copyrights and violated their rights of publicity.

column1: Judge William Orrick of the Northern District of California issued a ruling in October 2023 that significantly shaped the litigation landscape. He dismissed most claims but allowed certain direct infringement claims against Stability AI to proceed
column2: finding that the plaintiffs had adequately alleged that Stable Diffusion could produce images that are derivative works of their training data. The court rejected claims against Midjourney and DeviantArt at that stage and dismissed the DMCA claims with leave to amend. The ruling clarified that AI image generators are not insulated from copyright liability as a categorical matter
column3: while also narrowing the viable theories plaintiffs could pursue.

column1: A separate Getty Images lawsuit against Stability AI
column2: filed in both U.S. and UK courts
column3: made similar arguments with stronger evidence
column4: as Getty documented specific instances of Stable Diffusion outputs that included distorted versions of Getty's watermark
column5: suggesting direct copying of watermarked training images rather than abstract stylistic learning.

column1: The Authors Guild Class Action
In September 2023
column2: the <a href=""https://authorsguild.org/"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""Authors Guild"" data-summary=""The largest professional organization for published writers in the United States
column3: which filed a class action against OpenAI in 2023."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Authors Guild filed a class action against OpenAI in the Southern District of New York
column4: with more than 11
column5: 000 authors ultimately represented or associated with the action. The named plaintiffs included John Grisham
column6: Jodi Picoult
column7: George R.R. Martin
column8: and Jonathan Franzen
column9: lending the case considerable public profile. The complaint alleged that OpenAI trained GPT models on books obtained from shadow libraries including Library Genesis and other piracy repositories
column10: not just openly crawled web text.

column1: The authors' claims center on direct infringement for the training process itself and on market substitution
column2: arguing that ChatGPT can produce book summaries
column3: plot analyses
column4: and stylistically similar content that directly competes with the authors' works and reduces demand for them. The class seeks statutory damages
column5: which under copyright law can reach $150
column6: 000 per work for willful infringement. With potentially hundreds of thousands of works at issue
column7: the theoretical damages exposure runs into the tens of billions of dollars
column8: though courts regularly exercise discretion to reduce statutory damage awards at trial.

column1: Licensing Deals and the Emerging Market
While litigation proceeds
column2: some rights holders have chosen negotiation over confrontation. The Associated Press reached a licensing agreement with OpenAI in July 2023
column3: granting access to AP's text archive in exchange for an undisclosed fee. Reports at the time suggested the deal was structured partly around access and partly around ongoing content supply. Several other publishers
column4: including Axel Springer
column5: which owns Politico and Business Insider
column6: announced deals with OpenAI reportedly valued in the range of tens of millions of dollars over multi-year terms
column7: though neither party confirmed precise figures.

column1: These deals create an interesting tension in the litigation. If licensing is commercially viable
column2: defendants may find it harder to argue that unlicensed use causes no market harm. On the other hand
column3: defendants argue that reaching licensing deals for ongoing content supply is categorically different from licensing historical archives for training
column4: and that the existence of a market for one does not establish a market for the other. That distinction will likely matter significantly as courts analyze the fourth fair use factor.

column1: The pace of deal-making accelerated through 2024 and into 2025
column2: with news organizations calculating that litigation is expensive and uncertain while licensing provides immediate revenue. The litigation plaintiffs
column3: however
column4:
argue that licensing deals reached after the fact and under legal pressure cannot retroactively establish that pre-litigation training was lawful. That argument will likely define a central battleground in the cases that reach trial.

Loomis Wisconsin Supreme Court (2016); Dressel and Farid Science Advances 2018"	"COMPAS|AI sentencing|criminal justice|algorithmic bias|ProPublica"	"Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)"	"In 2016
column2:
ProPublica analyzed the COMPAS risk assessment tool used in criminal sentencing in Florida. The algorithm's developer disputed the findings. Both the data and the dispute are public record." "

American courts sentence millions of people each year. Judges weigh criminal history
column3: severity of offense
column4: mitigating factors
column5: and dozens of other variables before deciding how long someone spends in prison. Over the past two decades
column6: a parallel system has entered that process: algorithmic risk assessment tools that assign defendants numerical scores predicting their likelihood of reoffending. The most widely used of these tools is COMPAS
column7: and its track record has become one of the central controversies in criminal justice technology.

column1: What COMPAS Is and How It Works
COMPAS stands for Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions.
column2: the company that develops and sells the COMPAS risk assessment tool."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Northpointe
column3: now rebranded as Equivant
column4: developed the tool and markets it to courts
column5: probation departments
column6: and parole boards across the United States. The instrument consists of 137 questions covering a defendant's criminal history
column7: social environment
column8: age
column9: education
column10: finances
column11: family background
column12: and attitudes toward criminal behavior. Defendants answer most questions directly
column13: sometimes through an interview administered by a court officer.

column1: The tool produces several scores
column2: but the most consequential is the general recidivism risk score
column3: rated on a scale from one to ten. A score of one through four typically places a defendant in the low-risk category. Five through seven indicates medium risk. Eight through ten marks someone as high risk. Courts and parole boards use these scores in presentence investigation reports
column4: parole hearings
column5: and decisions about supervision intensity. The tool does not technically recommend a sentence
column6: but scores appear in documents judges read before handing down punishment.

column1: Northpointe does not publish the algorithm underlying COMPAS. The weights assigned to each of the 137 questions
column2: the mathematical formula that converts answers into a score
column3: and the validation data behind the model remain proprietary. Defendants subject to these scores cannot inspect the methodology that produced them. That opacity sits at the center of nearly every legal and statistical challenge the tool has faced.

column1: The ProPublica Investigation
In May 2016
column2: ProPublica published a data-driven investigation that changed the national conversation about COMPAS. Reporters Julia Angwin
column3: Jeff Larson
column4: Surya Mattu
column5: and Lauren Kirchner obtained COMPAS scores for roughly 7
column6: 000 defendants in Broward County
column7: Florida
column8: then tracked those individuals over two years to see who actually reoffended.

column1: CB Factoid: ProPublica's ""Machine Bias"" investigation
column2: published May 23
column4: analyzed COMPAS scores for 7
column5: 214 defendants in Broward County
column6: Florida
column7: and found that Black defendants were nearly twice as likely as white defendants to be falsely flagged as future criminals (45 percent vs. 24 percent false positive rate) (Angwin
column8: Larson
column9: Mattu
column10: and Kirchner
column11: ""Machine Bias
column12: ProPublica
column13: May 23

column1: The findings were pointed. Among defendants who did not reoffend
column2: Black defendants were nearly twice as likely as white defendants to receive a high-risk score. The false positive rate for Black defendants reached 45 percent compared to 24 percent for white defendants. White defendants who did go on to reoffend were more often labeled low risk than their Black counterparts. The investigation framed this asymmetry as a racial bias embedded in the tool's outputs.

column1: ProPublica also documented individual cases that illustrated the statistical pattern. A teenager convicted of riding a stolen bicycle received a high-risk score. A middle-aged white defendant with prior armed robbery convictions received a low-risk score. The piece ran under the headline ""Machine Bias"" and drew immediate responses from legal scholars
column2: technologists
column3: and the tool's manufacturer.

column1: Northpointe's Response
Northpointe did not accept ProPublica's framing. The company published a detailed rebuttal arguing that ProPublica had applied the wrong definition of fairness to evaluate the tool.
CB Factoid: Northpointe's rebuttal to ProPublica
column2: published July 8
column4: argued that COMPAS satisfies the statistical criterion of ""predictive parity
column5: meaning that among defendants who scored high risk
column6: Black and white defendants reoffended at similar rates
column7: and that ProPublica's false positive framing conflated different mathematical definitions of fairness (Dieterich
column8: Mendoza
column9: and Brennan
column10: ""COMPAS Risk Scales: Demonstrating Accuracy
column11: Equity
column12: and Predictive Parity
column13: Northpointe Inc.
column14: July 8

column1: Northpointe's central argument relied on predictive parity. Among all defendants who scored high risk
column2: Black and white defendants reoffended at approximately the same rates. In other words
column3: a score of eight meant roughly the same thing regardless of race. The company argued this demonstrated that the tool performed equitably across racial groups.

column1: The dispute exposed a genuine mathematical problem. Computer scientists Chouldechova and Kleinberg
column2: working independently
column3: demonstrated around the same time that predictive parity and equal false positive rates cannot both be satisfied simultaneously when base rates of recidivism differ between groups. Because Black defendants in Broward County reoffended at higher rates than white defendants
column4: a tool calibrated for predictive parity will necessarily produce higher false positive rates for the group with the higher base rate. Both ProPublica and Northpointe were measuring real things. They were measuring different things. Which measurement reflects fairness is a normative question
column5: not a statistical one.

column1: What Humans Can Do With Two Questions

column2: researchers Julia Dressel and Hany Farid published a study in Science Advances that approached COMPAS from a different direction. Rather than debating which fairness metric applied
column3: they asked whether the algorithm performed better than ordinary human judgment.

column1: CB Factoid: Dressel and Farid's study
column2: ""The Accuracy
column3: Fairness
column4: and Limits of Predicting Recidivism
column5: published January 17
column7: in Science Advances
column8: found that untrained online volunteers predicting recidivism using brief case descriptions achieved 67 percent accuracy
column9: matching COMPAS's 65 to 67 percent accuracy
column10: and that a simple two-variable linear model using only age and prior convictions performed comparably to the full 137-question tool (Dressel and Farid
column11: Science Advances
column12: Vol. 4
column13: No. 1
column14: January 17

column1: Dressel and Farid recruited untrained volunteers through an online platform. They gave each volunteer a brief description of a defendant's criminal record and asked them to predict whether that person would reoffend within two years. The volunteers had no special training in criminology or statistics. They used no algorithm. They achieved roughly 67 percent accuracy
column2: which matched COMPAS's own reported accuracy of 65 to 67 percent on the same dataset.

column1: The second finding was more striking. The researchers built a simple logistic regression model using only two variables: the defendant's age and the number of prior convictions. That minimal model matched the performance of the full 137-question COMPAS instrument. Adding the remaining 135 questions produced no meaningful improvement in predictive accuracy. The study suggested that COMPAS's complexity does not translate into superior predictions. It also suggested that the appearance of scientific rigor
column2: generated by 137 questions and a proprietary algorithm
column3: may create false confidence in outputs that a simpler and more transparent method could replicate.

column1: State v. Loomis and the Legal Question
While researchers debated COMPAS's accuracy and fairness
column2: a Wisconsin defendant challenged its use in court. Eric Loomis received a six-year prison sentence after a judge consulted his COMPAS score. Loomis argued that relying on a proprietary algorithm violated his due process rights because he could not examine or challenge the methodology that contributed to his sentence.

column1: CB Factoid: In State v. Loomis
column2: decided July 13
column4: the Wisconsin Supreme Court upheld the use of COMPAS scores in sentencing on a 6-to-1 vote
column5: ruling that the defendant's due process rights were not violated because judges used the score as one factor among many and were not bound by it
column6: even though the court acknowledged that defendants cannot access or challenge the proprietary algorithm (State v. Loomis
column8: Wisconsin Supreme Court
column9: July 13

column1: The Wisconsin Supreme Court ruled against Loomis in a 6-to-1 decision. The majority held that COMPAS did not violate due process because the sentencing judge used the score as one factor among many and was not bound by it. The court noted that defendants could still challenge their underlying criminal history
column2: which feeds into the score. Because the judge retained independent discretion
column3: the algorithm did not unconstitutionally determine the outcome.

column1: The decision drew criticism from legal scholars who argued the court sidestepped the core problem. A defendant cannot meaningfully challenge a score if the formula generating it is secret. Knowing your criminal history is accurate does not tell you whether the algorithm weighted that history fairly. The court acknowledged the opacity issue but declined to find it constitutionally fatal. The United States Supreme Court declined to review the case
column2: leaving the Wisconsin ruling as the most prominent judicial statement on COMPAS and due process.

column1: Current Use and Emerging Regulations

COMPAS and similar tools remain in active use. The Brennan Center for Justice has tracked risk assessment adoption across more than half of U.S. states. These tools appear at pretrial detention hearings
column2: sentencing
column3: parole hearings
column4: and decisions about prison programming. COMPAS is one of several instruments in use. The Arnold Foundation's Public Safety Assessment
column5: the Level of Service Inventory
column6: and the Ohio Risk Assessment System operate in various jurisdictions. Each carries its own methodological debates.

column1: Regulatory responses have been uneven. Vermont passed legislation requiring courts to disclose when risk assessments are used and to provide defendants information about the instrument. New York and California have debated similar transparency requirements. Some jurisdictions have moved toward restricting or banning the use of risk scores in pretrial detention specifically
column2: responding to concerns that algorithmic recommendations may entrench existing disparities in who gets detained before trial.

column1: The federal government has not imposed uniform standards. The National Institute of Justice has funded research into risk tool validity but has not mandated disclosure of proprietary methodology. The absence of federal standards means defendants face widely different levels of transparency depending on which state prosecuted them.

The underlying tension in this debate is not purely technical. Proponents of risk assessment argue that algorithmic scores reduce reliance on individual judicial bias and produce more consistent outcomes. Critics respond that algorithms codify historical patterns of over-policing and unequal prosecution
column2: producing scores that reflect systemic inequality rather than individual danger. Dressel and Farid's finding that two variables perform as well as 137 raises a harder question: if the tool's predictive power rests almost entirely on age and prior convictions
column3: then the additional complexity generates the appearance of rigor without delivering its substance. That appearance may make scores harder to question
column4:
not easier to trust.

The statute is 26 words. Here is what it actually says and how courts have interpreted it." "

Section 230: The 26 Words That Built the Internet and the Debate Over Tearing Them Down
Few legal provisions have shaped modern life as quietly or as consequentially as Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. Enacted in 1996
column2: the statute runs to several subsections
column3: but its operational core fits in a single sentence. That sentence determined whether today's social media platforms
column4: comment sections
column5: review sites
column6: and online marketplaces could exist in their current form. It also determined
column7: critics argue
column8: whether platforms could escape accountability for enabling harassment
column9: radicalization
column10: and child exploitation. Understanding what Section 230 actually says
column11: what it does not say
column12: and what courts have done with it is prerequisite to any serious evaluation of proposed reforms.

column1: The Actual Text

Section 230(c)(1) reads: ""No provider or user of an interactive computer service shall be treated as the publisher or speaker of any information provided by another information content provider."" That is the core immunity. Twenty-six words. They establish that a platform hosting third-party content cannot be held liable as though it had authored that content itself.
CB Factoid: Section 230(c)(1) of the Communications Decency Act
column2: codified at 47 U.S.C. § 230(c)(1)
column3: states in full: ""No provider or user of an interactive computer service shall be treated as the publisher or speaker of any information provided by another information content provider."" The provision was enacted as part of the Telecommunications Act of 1996
column5: signed February 8

column1: Section 230(c)(2) adds what courts and scholars call the Good Samaritan clause. It provides that no provider or user of an interactive computer service shall be held liable ""on account of any action voluntarily taken in good faith to restrict access to or availability of material that the provider or user considers to be obscene
column2: lewd
column3: lascivious
column4: filthy
column5: excessively violent
column6: harassing
column7: or otherwise objectionable
column8: whether or not such material is constitutionally protected."" This subsection solves a specific problem: without it
column9: a platform that tried to moderate harmful content could paradoxically incur more liability than one that did nothing
column10: because moderation demonstrated awareness of the content. The Good Samaritan clause removed that disincentive.

column1: The statutory definition of ""interactive computer service"" is broad: ""any information service
column2: system
column3: or access software provider that provides or enables computer access by multiple users to a computer server."" Courts have applied this to cover web hosts
column4: social media companies
column5: search engines
column6: and online marketplaces. The definition of ""information content provider
column7: meaning the entity whose liability is blocked
column8: covers ""any person or entity that is responsible
column9: in whole or in part
column10: for the creation or development of information.""

column1: What Section 230 Does Not Cover

Section 230(e) contains explicit carve-outs that the public debate frequently ignores. Section 230(e)(1) states that nothing in the provision ""shall be construed to impair the enforcement of section 223 or 231 of this title
column2: chapter 71 (relating to obscenity) or 110 (relating to sexual exploitation of children) of title 18
column3: or any other Federal criminal statute."" Federal criminal law
column4: in other words
column5: applies fully. The Department of Justice can prosecute platforms for facilitating child sexual abuse material under existing federal statutes without any Section 230 barrier.

column1: Section 230(e)(2) carves out intellectual property law. A platform cannot invoke Section 230 to defeat a copyright infringement claim. That protection comes separately through the Digital Millennium Copyright Act's notice-and-takedown system. Section 230(e)(3) preempts state law that would impose liability inconsistent with the provision
column2: but Section 230(e)(5)
column3: added by the Fight Online Sex Trafficking Act in 2018
column4: creates an additional carve-out for federal and state sex trafficking enforcement. Platforms are therefore not immunized from prosecution or civil suits related to sex trafficking under federal law or under state law if the conduct violates the federal trafficking statute.

column1: Courts have also held that Section 230 does not protect a platform that becomes a co-developer of illegal content. If a platform structures its service to encourage illegal content
column2: it may lose immunity by crossing the line from passive host to active content creator.

column1: Zeran v. AOL: The Foundational Case
CB Factoid: In Zeran v. America Online
column2: Inc.
column4: the United States Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit held that Section 230 bars ""any cause of action that would make service providers liable for information originating with a third-party user of the service
column5: establishing the broad immunity interpretation that has governed Section 230 jurisprudence since.

column1: Kenneth Zeran was an ordinary American who became the target of anonymous harassment in 1995. An unknown user posted notices on AOL bulletin boards advertising T-shirts with offensive slogans about the Oklahoma City bombing
column2: listing Zeran's home phone number as the contact. Zeran received death threats. He called AOL repeatedly asking for removal
column3: but the posts kept appearing from new accounts. He sued AOL for negligence in failing to act.

column1: The Fourth Circuit ruled against Zeran and established the interpretive framework that has governed Section 230 ever since. Judge J. Harvie Wilkinson III wrote that Section 230 creates ""federal immunity to any cause of action that would make service providers liable for information originating with a third-party user of the service."" The court rejected Zeran's argument that a distinction existed between publisher liability and distributor liability. Under traditional tort law
column2: a distributor like a bookstore could be held liable for harmful content once given notice of its existence. The Fourth Circuit held that Section 230 eliminated this distinction entirely. Once a provider qualifies under Section 230(c)(1)
column3: it cannot be sued as publisher or distributor
column4: even with notice of specific harmful content.

column1: The
column2: 129 F.3d 327 (4th Cir. 1997)"" data-summary=""The Fourth Circuit's foundational ruling establishing broad Section 230 immunity for platforms hosting third-party content."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Zeran decision's breadth became the template for decades of litigation. Courts across circuits adopted it. Its reasoning allowed platforms to grow at scale without staffing legal teams to vet every user post. Critics have argued for just as long that the decision read the statute too broadly and that Congress never intended to eliminate distributor liability.

column1: Gonzalez v. Google: The Supreme Court Declines to Narrow the Statute
CB Factoid: In Gonzalez v. Google LLC
column3: the Supreme Court vacated the Ninth Circuit's judgment and remanded without ruling on the merits of Section 230's application to algorithmic recommendations
column4: leaving the foundational interpretation of the statute intact and declining to adopt the petitioners' theory that recommendation algorithms constitute speech by the platform itself.

column1: The family of Nohemi Gonzalez
column2: killed in the 2015 Paris ISIS attacks
column3: sued Google on the theory that YouTube's recommendation algorithm actively promoted ISIS recruitment videos to susceptible users. Their argument was that recommendations were not passive hosting but affirmative speech by the platform
column4: taking the activity outside Section 230 immunity. If the Supreme Court accepted this theory
column5: every algorithmic curation decision would potentially expose platforms to liability.

column1: The
column2: 598 U.S. 617 (2023)"" data-summary=""Supreme Court opinion vacating the Ninth Circuit's Section 230 ruling and remanding without resolving the algorithmic recommendation immunity question."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Supreme Court's 2023 opinion declined to reach that question directly. The Court vacated the Ninth Circuit's judgment on other grounds related to the Anti-Terrorism Act and remanded. In a concurrence
column3: Justice Thomas expressed skepticism about the broad immunity courts had built from the statute's text. But the Court issued no ruling narrowing Section 230's application to recommendations. The effect was to leave existing doctrine intact
column4: which disappointed both petitioners seeking accountability and platforms seeking definitive endorsement of their immunity.

column1: Reform Proposals
Congress has produced a substantial volume of reform legislation
column2: most of which has not advanced past committee hearings. The major proposals cluster around three approaches.

column1: The
column3: 118th Congress"" data-summary=""Senate bill proposing to condition Section 230 immunity on platform compliance with best practices for detecting and reporting child sexual abuse material."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">EARN IT Act
column4: introduced in multiple Congress sessions and reintroduced in 2023
column5: proposes conditioning Section 230 immunity on compliance with best practices established by a national commission for detecting and reporting child sexual abuse material. Critics
column6: including encryption advocates
column7: argue the bill's best practices could require platforms to weaken end-to-end encryption
column8: effectively mandating backdoor government access. Supporters argue that the existing federal criminal carve-out is insufficient because it does not create civil incentives for platforms to detect CSAM proactively.

column1: The STOP CSAM Act
column2: introduced in 2023 by Senator Dick Durbin
column3: takes a more targeted approach. It would create an explicit civil right of action allowing survivors of child sexual abuse to sue platforms that knowingly host CSAM or that take actions to facilitate its distribution. Unlike EARN IT
column4: it does not condition general immunity on commission-certified best practices but narrows the existing carve-out to create a usable private right of action.

column1: The PACT Act
column2: or Platform Accountability and Consumer Transparency Act
column3: addresses a different failure mode. It would require platforms to publish clear content moderation policies
column4: process user complaints within defined timeframes
column5: and submit to annual audits. It does not eliminate Section 230 immunity but attaches procedural obligations to retaining it. Platforms that fail to follow their own stated policies lose immunity for decisions made in violation of those policies.

column1: The Empirical Question
Whether Section 230 was
column2: on net
column3: beneficial to society is genuinely contested and the empirical record is incomplete. Economists and legal scholars who defend the provision point to counterfactual analysis: without broad immunity
column4: the user-generated content model that produced Wikipedia
column5: Craigslist
column6: Yelp
column7: Reddit
column8: and social media platforms would have been legally untenable. The economic value generated is substantial. A 2019 analysis by the Chamber of Commerce estimated that internet platform activity contributed significantly to GDP
column9: though the causal attribution to Section 230 specifically is difficult to isolate.

column1: Critics point to a different set of documented harms.
column2: 'The State of Online Harassment
column3: ' January 2021"" data-summary=""Pew Research Center survey finding that 41 percent of Americans have experienced online harassment
column4: with women and people of color reporting the highest rates."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Pew Research Center data from 2021 found that 41 percent of Americans have experienced online harassment. Studies on algorithmic amplification of extremist content
column5: while methodologically contested
column6: suggest that platform recommendation systems can accelerate radicalization. The National Center for Missing and Exploited Children received over 32 million reports of suspected child sexual exploitation in 2022
column7: the vast majority originating from online platforms.

column1: Neither data set resolves the legal question
column2: because Section 230's scope is a policy choice about how to allocate liability
column3: not a technical conclusion from empirical inputs. The statute could be narrowed to create liability for specific categories of demonstrated harm without eliminating the broader hosting immunity that enabled the internet economy. Whether Congress has the political will and technical precision to achieve that narrowing is a separate question from whether the statute deserves reform at all.

column1: Section 230 remains
column2: 28 years after enactment
column3: the legal infrastructure of the internet. The debate over it is not really about 26 words. It is about who bears the cost when scale creates harm
column4:
and whether the architecture of law built on those words was ever designed to answer that question.

column1: The regulatory response across Europe reflects a different legal framework for workplace surveillance than exists in most U.S. jurisdictions. American workers generally have fewer statutory protections against employer monitoring
column2: leaving labor advocates to rely primarily on NLRA protections
column3: which cover organizing-related retaliation but not surveillance as such. European workers can challenge monitoring systems on proportionality grounds under GDPR
column4: giving regulators authority to examine whether the scope of data collection is justified by legitimate business purposes.

column1: What the combined documentation from patents
column2: NLRB complaints
column3: investigative journalism
column4: and regulatory decisions describes is a comprehensive architecture. Amazon tracks hand position
column5: scanning rate
column6: break duration
column7: eye movement
column8: driving behavior
column9: and union activity across its workforce. Automated systems process that data and initiate disciplinary action. Human managers receive outputs rather than making independent assessments. Workers receive decisions and must navigate appeals processes to contest them. This structure is not unique to Amazon
column10: but Amazon has implemented it at a scale and with a technical specificity that has made it the primary reference point in public debates about algorithmic management
column11: worker privacy
column12:
and the future of labor in automated logistics systems.

CB-000230 "Digital Redlining: FCC Data on Broadband Access by Income and Race" "digital-redlining-fcc-broadband-access-income-race-data-documented" "technology" "published" "91" "Digital Redlining FCC Broadband Access by Race | CB-000230" "FCC Broadband Deployment Report 2024; National Digital Inclusion Alliance mapping; The Markup ""Dollars to Megabits"" investigation 2022; FCC Form 477 data" "digital redlining|broadband|FCC|digital divide|race" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "The FCC collects data on broadband deployment by census tract. The pattern of access by income and by the racial composition of neighborhoods has been documented by researchers and government investigators." "

The term ""redlining"" originates from the mid-20th century practice of denying mortgage loans and financial services to residents of neighborhoods drawn in red on government maps
column2: almost always communities of color. Digital redlining follows the same structural logic. Internet service providers deploy fiber optic cables
column3: DOCSIS 3.1 infrastructure
column4: and gigabit-capable networks in wealthier ZIP codes while routing older
column5: slower copper telephone lines and degraded cable infrastructure through lower-income and minority neighborhoods. Prices often remain nearly identical across both service tiers. Residents in lower-income areas pay comparable monthly bills for connections that cannot support remote work
column6: telehealth
column7: or online education at functional speeds.

column1: The concept entered federal policy vocabulary partly through the work of researcher Christopher Mitchell at the Institute for Local Self-Reliance and through community organizers in cities like Cleveland and Detroit who documented the pattern block by block. The argument is not that ISPs explicitly target race. The argument is that infrastructure investment decisions correlate strongly with neighborhood income and racial composition
column2: producing outcomes that are discriminatory in effect regardless of stated intent.

column1: CB Factoid: The Institute for Local Self-Reliance's ""Community Broadband Networks"" initiative documented that as of 2023
column2: at least 19 states maintained laws restricting or outright prohibiting municipal broadband networks
column3: with many of those laws passed after direct lobbying by AT&T
column4: Comcast
column5: and Charter Communications (Institute for Local Self-Reliance
column6: ""Profiles of Restrictive State Laws
column7: Community Broadband Networks Initiative
column8: updated 2023).

column1: For years
column2: the Federal Communications Commission collected broadband availability data through what it called Form 477. Providers self-reported their coverage areas
column3: and the FCC used those reports to publish national maps and make policy decisions about where federal funding should flow. The problem was structural. Form 477 treated an entire census block as served if any single address within that block had access to broadband. A provider could run fiber to one house on a long rural road and mark the entire census block as covered. Urban census blocks with high populations remained similarly overcounted. ISPs had financial incentives to overclaim coverage because unserved areas attracted federal subsidies that threatened their monopoly positions.

column1: The FCC began replacing Form 477 with a new system called the Broadband Data Collection
column2: anchored by what it calls the Broadband Serviceable Location Fabric. The Fabric attempts to map every broadband-serviceable location in the country and require providers to report availability at the individual address level rather than the census block level. The results were striking. Millions of locations that Form 477 designated as served turned out to lack any broadband access at all. The FCC's updated maps
column3: released in phases beginning in late 2022 and refined through 2024
column4: revealed that previous coverage estimates were substantially inflated across rural and urban areas alike.

column1: CB Factoid: The FCC's Broadband Deployment Report released in January 2024 found that approximately 24.3 million Americans lacked access to fixed broadband at speeds of 25 Mbps download and 3 Mbps upload
column2: a significant revision from earlier Form 477-based estimates that had placed the figure closer to 14.5 million (FCC
column3: ""2024 Broadband Deployment Report
column4: GN Docket No. 23-388
column5: Federal Communications Commission
column6: January 2024).

column1: The mapping problem matters because federal dollars follow federal maps. If a neighborhood appears covered in FCC data
column2: it cannot receive infrastructure funding under most federal programs. Inaccurate coverage data meant that underserved urban communities of color
column3: which technically had some form of broadband service available
column4: could not access funds designed to improve their connections. The revised Fabric data has started correcting this
column5: though advocates argue the challenge method for disputing provider-submitted data still places too much burden on individual residents.

column1: While federal data collection improved
column2: journalism provided some of the clearest documentation of the speed and price disparity problem. In 2022
column3: <a href=""https://themarkup.org/still-loading/2022/10/19/att-and-verizon-offer-slower-internet-speeds-in-lower-income-areas"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""The Markup
column4: 'Big Tech Is Pushing States to Allow Them to Harvest Your Data. Don't Let Them.'"" data-summary=""The Markup's 2022 investigation tested internet speeds at 800
column5: 000 locations and found major ISPs consistently offering slower service in lower-income neighborhoods at similar prices."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">The Markup published an investigation that analyzed speed test data from more than 800
column6: 000 residential locations across the United States. Reporters examined offerings from AT&T
column7: Verizon
column8: and CenturyLink in cities including Los Angeles
column9: Detroit
column10: Cleveland
column11: and New York. They compared the maximum speeds available to customers in lower-income neighborhoods against the speeds available in higher-income neighborhoods within the same metropolitan area
column12: controlling for price.

column1: CB Factoid: The Markup's October 2022 investigation
column2: ""AT&T and Verizon Offer Slower Internet Speeds in Lower-Income Areas
column3: analyzed 800
column4: 000 residential internet plan offers and found AT&T customers in low-income areas were offered plans with maximum speeds up to 5.5 times slower than customers in higher-income areas at the same price point

column1: The findings were specific and quantifiable. In Detroit
column2: AT&T offered customers in lower-income census tracts plans capped at much slower speeds than customers in wealthier parts of the same city paid for comparable monthly rates. The disparity did not appear in fiber coverage areas
column3: where the company had upgraded infrastructure. It appeared precisely in areas where AT&T had not deployed fiber and continued operating its aging copper DSL network. The Markup found the pattern repeated across multiple ISPs and multiple cities
column4: suggesting a systemic infrastructure investment strategy rather than isolated pricing decisions.

column1: The National Digital Inclusion Alliance has produced city-level mapping that complements The Markup's findings. NDIA's analysis
column2: drawing on American Community Survey data and ISP offer data
column3: identified specific neighborhoods within cities like Cleveland
column4: Pittsburgh
column5: and Baltimore as facing the most severe combinations of low adoption rates and poor service availability. In Cleveland's Glenville neighborhood and Detroit's east side
column6: NDIA documented adoption rates below 50 percent alongside evidence of limited ISP infrastructure investment. These neighborhoods share demographic characteristics: majority Black populations and median household incomes well below the metropolitan average.

column1: NDIA's framework emphasizes that low adoption does not simply reflect consumer choice. When the only available service costs $80 per month for 25 Mbps on copper infrastructure while wealthier nearby neighborhoods receive gigabit fiber for $70 monthly
column2: the price-to-performance ratio creates a rational barrier to subscription. Digital inclusion advocates argue that treating adoption as a demand problem ignores the supply-side infrastructure discrimination that shapes available choices.

column1: Congress responded to documented broadband gaps with the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act
column2: signed in November 2021. The law included $65 billion for broadband
column3: the largest federal broadband investment in history. The primary vehicle is the <a href=""https://broadbandusa.ntia.gov/internet-for-all/bead"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""NTIA BEAD Program"" data-summary=""The NTIA administers the $42.45 billion BEAD program
column4: distributing funds to states for broadband deployment in unserved and underserved locations."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Broadband Equity
column5: Access
column6: and Deployment program
column7: which allocates $42.45 billion to states based on the number of unserved and underserved locations identified in the FCC Fabric. Additional funding flows through the Affordable Connectivity Program
column8: Digital Equity Act programs
column9: and ReConnect grants for rural areas.

column1: The BEAD program requires states to prioritize deployment to unserved locations first
column2: then underserved locations
column3: then community anchor institutions. It sets a minimum speed threshold of 100 Mbps download and 20 Mbps upload for funded projects
column4: with a preference for networks capable of gigabit speeds. These provisions directly address the infrastructure gap that creates digital redlining conditions.

column1: ISPs engaged in sustained lobbying over the program's affordability requirements. The BEAD Notice of Funding Opportunity requires states to ensure funded providers offer low-cost options to qualifying households
column2: but it grants states flexibility in how to define and enforce that requirement. Major carriers lobbied against more prescriptive federal price controls
column3: arguing that local market conditions should determine pricing and that strict affordability mandates would discourage private investment. Consumer advocacy groups pushed for mandatory low-cost tiers with specific price caps
column4: arguing that building infrastructure without addressing price would replicate existing subscription gaps in newly served areas.

column1: The tension between infrastructure deployment and affordability enforcement reflects a broader disagreement about whether digital redlining is primarily a coverage problem
column2: a price problem
column3: or both simultaneously. Advocates argue it is both
column4: and that deploying fiber to underserved neighborhoods while allowing ISPs to price service beyond the reach of low-income residents simply builds new infrastructure into an unchanged access problem.

column1: Municipal broadband represents one alternative to reliance on private ISPs. Chattanooga
column2: Tennessee became the most documented American example. The city's publicly owned Electric Power Board launched a fiber network in 2010 that now covers the entire city with symmetrical gigabit service. The EPB network charges rates competitive with private ISPs and extends service to public housing developments and low-income neighborhoods as part of its operational mandate rather than as a separate charitable program. Chattanooga's network has been studied extensively as evidence that public ownership can eliminate within-city infrastructure disparities.

column1: The Chattanooga model provoked immediate responses from private ISPs. AT&T and Comcast lobbied the Tennessee state legislature to restrict the EPB's ability to expand its service territory beyond the city limits. Tennessee passed a law in 2011 limiting municipal utilities from offering broadband outside their existing electric service territory. The restriction prevented EPB from serving surrounding rural counties that wanted access to its network. Tennessee is one of the 19 states ILSR identified as maintaining restrictive municipal broadband laws
column2: most passed under direct carrier lobbying pressure.

column1: The pattern repeated in state after state. When cities in North Carolina
column2: Arkansas
column3: and Wisconsin moved toward municipal broadband
column4: private ISPs funded campaigns and lobbied legislators to prohibit or restrict public networks before they could launch. The lobbying strategy treated municipal broadband not as a policy dispute but as a competitive threat requiring legislative elimination. ILSR's documentation of these lobbying campaigns provides some of the clearest evidence that ISPs view public network competition as a direct threat to their ability to maintain infrastructure investment disparities without accountability.

column1: The BEAD program does not resolve this tension. States with restrictive municipal broadband laws may use federal funds to expand private ISP coverage while blocking the public network alternatives that advocates argue are most likely to eliminate the price and equity gaps that define digital redlining. Whether $65 billion in federal investment restructures broadband access or subsidizes existing ISPs to replicate their current infrastructure strategies in previously unserved areas will depend on state-level implementation decisions made over the next several years
column2:
watched closely by the communities that have waited longest for functional connectivity.

Clearview AI (Illinois); GAO facial recognition report 2021" "Clearview AI|facial recognition|surveillance|law enforcement|privacy" "Fri Apr 03 2026 00:00:00 GMT+0000 (Coordinated Universal Time)" "Clearview AI built a facial recognition database of more than 30 billion images scraped from the public internet. Federal agencies contracted with the company. The contracts and the legal challenges are documented." "

Clearview AI built one of the most extensive facial recognition databases in existence by scraping billions of images from social media platforms
column2: news websites
column3: and public web pages without seeking permission from the people pictured or the platforms hosting those images. The company collected photographs from <a href=""https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/18/technology/clearview-privacy-facial-recognition.html"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""New York Times"" data-summary=""Investigative report on Clearview AI's data collection methods and law enforcement use."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Facebook
column4: LinkedIn
column5: YouTube
column6: Venmo
column7: and millions of other websites
column8: attaching those images to searchable profiles. Users who upload a photograph to Clearview's system receive a grid of matching images pulled from across the internet
column9: along with links to the original source pages. The company did not notify individuals that their faces had been added to the database
column10: did not obtain consent
column11: and did not offer a straightforward mechanism for removal.

column1: Facebook
column2: Google
column3: LinkedIn
column4: and Venmo each sent cease-and-desist letters to Clearview AI after the company's practices became public in early 2020. The platforms argued that scraping violated their terms of service. Clearview disputed that characterization
column5: arguing that publicly accessible images are fair game under the First Amendment. That legal theory has faced significant scrutiny from courts
column6: regulators
column7: and civil liberties organizations in the years since.

column1: The database grew to contain more than three billion images when the company first became widely known. Later estimates put the figure significantly higher. Clearview's founder
column2: Hoan Ton-That
column3: argued the tool gave law enforcement an unprecedented ability to identify suspects
column4: missing persons
column5: and victims. Critics noted that the same capability
column6: deployed at scale
column7: could enable mass surveillance of ordinary people who had no involvement in any criminal activity.

column1: In February 2020
column2: BuzzFeed News published a detailed analysis of a leaked Clearview client list that named thousands of organizations worldwide. The document showed accounts associated with the FBI
column3: the Department of Justice
column4: Immigration and Customs Enforcement
column5: the Drug Enforcement Administration
column6: U.S. Customs and Border Protection
column7: the Secret Service
column8: and dozens of state and local police departments. Many of these agencies had accessed the system on a trial basis

column1: Federal contracting records and investigative reporting filled in additional details about the financial relationships between Clearview and federal agencies. ICE signed contracts with Clearview AI worth hundreds of thousands of dollars
column2: using the system as part of immigration enforcement operations. The <a href=""https://theintercept.com/2020/08/26/clearview-ai-ice-secret-service-dhs/"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""The Intercept"" data-summary=""Report on federal agency contracts with Clearview AI
column3: including ICE
