column3: was recorded and later transcribed. The full text is preserved in the archives of the Democratic National Committee and was reprinted in the Congressional Record by Representative Edith Green (D-OR) in 1964. Hamer stated: ""I question America. Is this America
column4: the land of the free and the home of the brave
column5: where we have to sleep with our telephones off the hooks because our lives be threatened daily
column6: because we want to live as decent human beings
column7: in America?""

column1: President Lyndon Johnson watched Hamer's testimony begin on live television from the White House. He called an unscheduled press conference at almost exactly the moment her remarks started. The major networks cut away to cover the president. NBC News chose to air Hamer's full testimony in its evening broadcast rather than replay Johnson's press conference
column2: which contained no news of substance. Johnson's maneuver failed to suppress the coverage entirely. Millions of Americans saw or heard Hamer's account of being beaten in a Mississippi jail for trying to vote. The political cost of that visibility fell on Johnson
column3: who had wanted to avoid a floor fight at the convention over the Mississippi delegation question.

column1: The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party arrived in Atlantic City with a specific and documented demand. They argued that the regular Mississippi Democratic Party delegation had been selected through a process that excluded Black voters through violence
column2: economic coercion
column3: and discriminatory registration practices. The MFDP held that its sixty-eight delegates

column1: The compromise the credentials committee offered
column2: brokered by Walter Mondale and Hubert Humphrey among others
column3: gave the MFDP two at-large seats with no voting rights and no assigned seats on the floor. The two seats would be honorary. The regular all-white Mississippi delegation would retain its seats. Hamer rejected the compromise publicly and without hesitation. She said
column4: ""We didn't come all this way for no two seats."" The regular Mississippi delegation walked out anyway. The MFDP members sat in their vacated seats until officials removed the chairs. The compromise satisfied no one and solved nothing.

column1: The Voting Rights Act passed Congress and was signed by Johnson on August 6
column2: 1965. The legislation addressed with precision the specific mechanisms that had kept Hamer and millions of others off voter rolls across the South. Section 4 of the act included a coverage formula that identified states and counties where literacy tests or similar devices had been used as conditions for voting and where voter registration or turnout had fallen below fifty percent in the 1964 presidential election. Mississippi qualified immediately under that formula. Sunflower County qualified. The act suspended literacy tests in covered jurisdictions.

column1: CB Factoid: Section 4(a) of the Voting Rights Act of 1965
column2: Public Law 89-110
column3: signed August 6
column5: suspended the use of any ""test or device"" as a qualification for voting in covered jurisdictions. The statute defined ""test or device"" to include literacy tests
column6: good character requirements
column7: and voucher systems. The Department of Justice published implementing regulations in 28 C.F.R. Part 51
column8: and the coverage determination for Mississippi was published in the Federal Register on August 7

column1: Section 6 of the act authorized the federal government to send examiners directly into covered counties to register voters
column2: bypassing state and local registrars entirely. This provision addressed exactly what had happened in Indianola in 1962. A federal examiner could not give Hamer an impossible constitutional interpretation test. A federal examiner could not fail her for reasons invented on the spot. The act also required covered jurisdictions to submit any changes to voting laws or procedures to the Department of Justice for preclearance before implementation
column3: a provision designed to stop states from inventing new barriers as fast as federal courts struck down old ones.

column1: The results in Mississippi were immediate and measurable. Black voter registration in Mississippi rose from approximately 6.7 percent of eligible Black voters in 1964 to approximately 59.8 percent by 1967
column2: according to Department of Justice historical records on the Voting Rights Act. Sunflower County
column3: the place that had evicted Hamer for attempting to register
column4: began electing Black officials within a decade. Hamer herself ran for the Mississippi State Senate in 1971
column5: losing narrowly in a race her supporters alleged was marred by irregularities.

column1: Hamer died on March 14
column3: from complications related to heart disease
column4: diabetes
column5: and the kidney damage she had sustained in the Winona jail. She was fifty-nine years old. She died in Mound Bayou
column6: Mississippi
column7: without significant financial resources. Her funeral was attended by Andrew Young
column8: Stokely Carmichael
column9: and Eleanor Holmes Norton
column10: among others. She was buried in Ruleville
column11: Sunflower County
column12: not far from where she had lived as a sharecropper.

column1: Sunflower County in 2025 remains one of the poorest counties in the United States
column2: with a population that is approximately 72 percent Black. Voting access in the county faces documented structural pressures. <a href=""https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/research-reports/voting-laws-roundup-2023"" class=""cb-factoid"" data-title=""Brennan Center for Justice"" data-summary=""Annual roundup tracking voting legislation across all fifty states
column3: including Mississippi restrictions enacted since 2020."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Mississippi enacted voter ID requirements that civil rights organizations have challenged as disproportionately burdening low-income and minority voters. The Supreme Court's 2013 decision in Shelby County v. Holder eliminated the preclearance requirement that had required Mississippi to obtain federal approval before changing voting rules
column4: removing a central enforcement mechanism of the 1965 act. Transportation to polling places in rural Sunflower County remains limited. The county has one early voting site.

column1: Hamer's documented impact on voting rights sits at an unusual intersection. She did not write the legislation. She did not argue the legal cases. She testified before a committee and was beaten in a jail cell and described both experiences on national television with accuracy and without theatrics. The specific provisions of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 correspond to the specific barriers she encountered. The literacy test she failed in Indianola became illegal. The registrar who turned her away lost his exclusive authority over voter enrollment. The chain from her attempt to register in 1962 to the statutory text of 1965 is not symbolic. It is documented
column2: traceable
column3:
and precise.

column1: HB 1467 and the Certified Media Specialist Requirement

Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed House Bill 1467 into law in March 2022. The legislation required that every book accessible to students in school libraries and classroom collections must first be reviewed and approved by a certified district employee with training in library media. Until that review occurred
column2: schools were required to make those materials inaccessible to students.

column1: The law created an immediate logistical crisis. Many Florida school districts did not employ enough certified media specialists to review their existing collections in any reasonable timeframe. In districts facing this shortage
column2: administrators faced a stark choice: leave books accessible and risk penalties
column3: or remove them from student reach until reviews could be completed.

column1: Several districts chose removal. In some schools
column2: teachers covered classroom library shelves with paper or fabric so students could not see or access books that had not yet cleared review. Images of paper-covered bookshelves circulated widely on social media and drew national press coverage. The Florida Department of Education issued guidance in early 2023 clarifying that books in classroom libraries needed to go through the review process
column3: which extended the scope of the law beyond school library collections to individual classroom bookshelves maintained by teachers.

column1: The Chilling Effect on Proactive Removals
Researchers and library advocates documented what they described as a significant chilling effect in Florida schools. This term refers to books removed by administrators or teachers proactively
column2: before any formal complaint was filed
column3: out of concern that the books might draw objections or create legal risk for the district or individual staff members.

column1: PEN America's methodology distinguished between books removed through formal challenge processes and books removed through administrative action without any documented complaint. column2: but PEN America and the American Library Association both documented patterns of preemptive removal in Florida districts.

column1: Teachers described self-censoring their classroom collections by removing books they anticipated could generate complaints
column2: even books they believed were educationally valuable. Librarians reported similar patterns
column3: pulling books they expected might be challenged before challengers identified them. This behavior extended the practical scope of the book removal campaign well beyond what formal complaint tallies alone captured.

column1: Legal Challenges and PEN America v. Escambia County
Legal challenges to Florida's book removal practices reached federal court. PEN America
column2: joined by authors and parents
column3: filed suit against Escambia County School District
column4: arguing that book removals violated the First Amendment rights of students and authors. The case
column5: PEN America v. Escambia County
column6: centered on whether school boards could remove books from libraries based on viewpoint without violating constitutional protections.

column1: Plaintiffs argued that Escambia County removed books because of their content and viewpoint
column2: particularly books addressing LGBTQ+ themes and race
column3: and that this constituted viewpoint discrimination prohibited by the First Amendment. They cited the Supreme Court's 1982 decision in Board of Education v. Pico
column4: which established that school boards cannot remove books from libraries simply because they disagree with the ideas the books contain.

column1: The district court dismissed the case in 2023
column2: finding that the plaintiffs lacked standing to bring the claims they asserted. The court did not reach the merits of the First Amendment arguments. PEN America pursued an appeal. The litigation joined several similar cases in other jurisdictions testing the boundaries of school board authority to remove library books and the constitutional limits on that authority.

column1: The legal landscape remained unsettled through 2024. No federal appellate court had issued a definitive ruling on the constitutionality of Florida's specific removal mechanisms
column2: leaving districts operating under state law requirements while First Amendment challenges worked through the courts. The volume of removals
column3: the documented chilling effect on teacher and librarian behavior
column4:
and the scale of the Duval County situation collectively positioned Florida as the central case study in national debates about the limits of school library censorship.

column1: The legal framework governing these disputes depends entirely on the public or private status of the institution. Public colleges and universities are state actors bound by the First Amendment under settled constitutional doctrine. A public university that disciplines a faculty member or cancels a speaker based on viewpoint commits a constitutional violation subject to federal court review and potential damages under 42 U.S.C. Section 1983. Private institutions have no equivalent constitutional obligation. They can and do restrict speech without triggering First Amendment scrutiny. Their obligations run instead through their own stated policies
column2: accreditation standards
column3: and whatever contractual commitments they make to faculty and students in employment agreements and student handbooks.

column1: This distinction produces a structural asymmetry in how cases resolve. Faculty at public universities who challenge discipline through litigation win at a higher rate than faculty at private institutions
column2: who must often rely on breach of contract claims or internal grievance procedures. FIRE has pursued litigation primarily against public institutions for this reason. The organization files amicus briefs and provides legal resources in private university cases but faces a more limited doctrinal toolkit.

column1: The Supreme Court's 2006 decision in Garcetti v. Ceballos adds a critical layer to the public employment analysis. Richard Ceballos
column2: a deputy district attorney in Los Angeles
column3: wrote a memo recommending dismissal of a criminal case after concluding that a law enforcement officer had lied in an affidavit. His supervisors retaliated against him. He sued
column4: claiming First Amendment protection. The Supreme Court ruled five to four that government employees do not receive First Amendment protection for speech made pursuant to their official duties. Justice Kennedy wrote for the majority that when public employees speak as employees rather than citizens on matters of public concern
column5: the government may discipline that speech without constitutional violation.

column1: CB Factoid: In Garcetti v. Ceballos
column3: the Supreme Court held that ""when public employees make statements pursuant to their official duties
column4: the employees are not speaking as citizens for First Amendment purposes
column5: and the Constitution does not insulate their communications from employer discipline
column6: a ruling that Justice Souter's dissent warned could threaten academic freedom by applying the same logic to faculty scholarship and teaching.

column1: The Garcetti holding creates a direct tension with academic freedom in public universities. Faculty who speak on academic subjects through their official roles
column2: meaning through teaching
column3: scholarship
column4: or public commentary in their professional capacity
column5: may fall outside First Amendment protection under a strict reading of Garcetti. Justice Kennedy acknowledged this concern in a footnote
column6: writing that the Court did not decide whether the analysis would apply in the same manner to a case involving speech related to scholarship or teaching. Lower courts have split on how to apply Garcetti to faculty speech. Some circuits apply a categorical academic freedom exception. Others apply the Garcetti test without modification. The Supreme Court has not resolved the circuit split.

column1: FIRE has argued in litigation and policy documents that the academic freedom exception must survive Garcetti to protect the university's function in a democratic society. The faculty cases documented between 2020 and 2024 frequently involve this unresolved question. A professor who posts on social media about policing in an individual capacity speaks as a citizen and receives fuller protection. A professor whose post draws on their scholarly expertise and institutional affiliation sits in a more ambiguous position. Administrators at several institutions have cited this ambiguity to justify initiating proceedings while avoiding clear constitutional confrontation. The result is a legal landscape where the boundaries of protected faculty speech at public universities remain contested
column2: documented case by case in FIRE's database
column3:
and litigated without a definitive Supreme Court resolution.

Social media users who believe platforms have wrongfully removed their content or suspended their accounts face a legal landscape that offers almost no viable paths to relief. Two separate doctrines combine to wall off most claims. Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act shields platforms from liability for content moderation decisions
column2: while the First Amendment's protection of editorial discretion gives private companies the right to decide what speech they host. Users squeezed between these two frameworks find that courts consistently dismiss their cases before reaching the merits.

column1: The result is a paradox that legal scholars have debated for years. Platforms powerful enough to silence a sitting president operate with fewer legal constraints on their speech decisions than a small-town newspaper editor. The newspaper analogy is not accidental. Courts have repeatedly applied

column3: establishing editorial discretion as a First Amendment right."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">the editorial discretion doctrine from traditional media to online platforms

column1: Section 230 adds another layer. The statute
column2: enacted in 1996
column3: immunizes platforms from civil liability for content moderation choices made in good faith. Courts have read this immunity broadly. Plaintiffs arguing that a platform wrongly removed their content have found that the statute blocks state tort claims
column4: breach of contract suits
column5: and various other theories. The combination leaves users with claims that sound compelling in ordinary language but fail at the threshold in federal court.

column3: those companies became state actors
column4: and the resulting removals violated the First Amendment. The case produced extensive discovery revealing that government officials had communicated frequently with platform trust and safety teams about content the administration considered misinformation.

The Court found that the plaintiffs could not demonstrate a sufficient causal link between the government communications and the specific content removals that harmed them. Platforms moderated content for their own reasons

column2: Florida

column1: Texas and Florida took a legislative approach to platform power. Both states enacted laws in 2021 that prohibited large social media companies from censoring users based on political viewpoint. The laws drew on an old regulatory concept: common carriers
column2: like telephone companies and railroads
column3: must serve all customers without discrimination. Proponents argued that platforms had become so dominant in public discourse that the same logic should apply to them.

column1: The laws produced a split in the circuits. The Eleventh Circuit struck down Florida's law as a violation of platforms' First Amendment rights to make editorial decisions. The Fifth Circuit upheld Texas's law. The Supreme Court took up both cases in
column2: LLC

column4: requiring lower courts to conduct more thorough facial challenge analysis before ruling on constitutionality."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">Moody v. NetChoice in 2024 and declined to rule definitively on constitutionality
column5: instead vacating and remanding for more thorough analysis. The Court's majority opinion signaled skepticism about laws that compel platforms to carry content against their editorial judgment
column6: but left final resolution to the lower courts.

column1: The common carrier theory faces a fundamental difficulty. Traditional common carriers provide fungible transportation or communication services. Social media platforms engage in curation
column2: ranking
column3: and recommendation. Courts applying the editorial discretion doctrine view those functions as expressive choices protected by the First Amendment
column4: not neutral conduit services that can be regulated like freight shipments.

column1: The EU Digital Services Act Comparison
European regulators took a different approach. The Digital Services Act
column2: which became fully applicable to very large online platforms in February 2024
column3: creates affirmative obligations that have no American equivalent. Platforms must assess and mitigate systemic risks from their services
column4: provide transparency about their recommendation algorithms
column5: allow users to opt out of profiling-based recommendations
column6: and submit to independent audits.

column2: adopted October 19
column3: 2022) requires online platforms to provide affected users with a clear and specific statement of reasons when their content is removed or their accounts are suspended
column4: including the factual and legal basis for the decision and information about available redress mechanisms (European Parliament and Council
column5: Digital Services Act

column1: Article 17's statement-of-reasons requirement is particularly significant. American users who receive a content removal notice typically get a brief reference to a community guideline. European users are entitled to a specific explanation of the factual basis for the decision and information about how to challenge it. This procedural transparency creates accountability that no American legal doctrine currently requires.
The DSA also establishes an out-of-court dispute settlement mechanism. Users can bring complaints to certified dispute resolution bodies
column2: giving them a practical avenue for challenging decisions that would be dismissed in American courts for lack of standing or failure to state a viable legal claim. European policymakers explicitly designed the framework around the recognition that leaving content moderation entirely to platform discretion produces outcomes that many users and governments consider unacceptable.

column1: American courts have shown little appetite for importing these concepts through judicial interpretation. Absent new legislation
column2: the framework in the United States will continue to function as it does now: Section 230 blocks liability claims
column3: the First Amendment protects editorial choices
column4: standing requirements filter out government coercion suits
column5:
and the common carrier alternative remains constitutionally contested. Users who believe their speech was wrongly suppressed retain the right to complain. They have very little else.

column1: Who Files Challenges Now
The composition of who files challenges shifted dramatically after 2021. Before that point
column2: the typical challenge came from an individual parent objecting to a specific book their child encountered in a specific classroom or school library. The complaints were local

column1: This matters for how libraries process complaints. Most library systems have formal reconsideration policies that require a review committee to evaluate each challenge individually
column2: assess the book's literary and educational merit
column3: and issue a written decision. A policy designed to handle a handful of individual complaints each year strains under hundreds of simultaneous submissions targeting dozens of titles. Some districts simply could not process challenges fast enough through their formal procedures and removed books temporarily pending review
column4: a practice that functioned as de facto removal in many cases.

column1: Most Challenged Books in 2023
CB Factoid: The ALA Office for Intellectual Freedom's 2023 list of most challenged books included ""Gender Queer"" by Maia Kobabe at number one for the third consecutive year
column2: followed by titles including ""All Boys Aren't Blue"" by George M. Johnson and ""This Book Is Gay"" by Juno Dawson. Source: American Library Association
column3: ""Top 10 Most Challenged Books of 2023
column4: Office for Intellectual Freedom
column5: March 2024.

column1: The 2023 top ten most challenged books reveal a consistent pattern when examined against their actual content and the stated grounds for challenge.

""Gender Queer"" by Maia Kobabe is a graphic memoir about the author's experience figuring out their gender identity and sexual orientation. Challengers cited sexual content and LGBTQ themes. The book was written specifically for LGBTQ young adults who may be navigating similar questions.

""All Boys Aren't Blue"" by George M. Johnson is a memoir covering Johnson's experiences as a Black queer man growing up in New Jersey. Challengers cited sexual content and LGBTQ themes. The book won multiple literary awards and is widely used in high school curricula discussing race and identity.
""This Book Is Gay"" by Juno Dawson is a nonfiction guide aimed at LGBTQ teenagers
column2: covering topics including relationships
column3: coming out
column4: and sexual health. Challengers cited sexual content and its presence in school libraries.

column1: ""Flamer"" by Mike Curato is a graphic novel about a teenage boy at summer camp questioning his sexuality. Challengers cited sexually explicit content and LGBTQ themes.
""The Perks of Being a Wallflower"" by Stephen Chbosky is a coming-of-age novel from 1999 about a teenager dealing with trauma
column2: depression
column3: and high school social life. It has appeared on challenged book lists for over two decades. Challengers cite sexual content
column4: drug use
column5: and offensive language.

column1: ""Lawn Boy"" by Jonathan Evison is a novel about a young Mexican-American man navigating economic hardship and sexuality. Challengers frequently described the book as containing content inappropriate for minors.
""The Kite Runner"" by Khaled Hosseini is a novel set in Afghanistan covering themes of friendship
column2: violence
column3: and redemption. It has been challenged primarily for a depiction of sexual assault and for its portrayal of Islam.

column1: ""Looking for Alaska"" by John Green is a young adult novel about students at a boarding school. It has appeared on challenged book lists since its 2005 publication. Challengers cite sexual content and drug use.
""Sold"" by Patricia McCormick is a novel about a Nepali girl trafficked into sexual slavery. Challengers cite sexual content. The book is frequently used in curricula addressing human trafficking.

""And Tango Makes Three"" by Justin Richardson and Peter Parnell is a children's picture book based on a true story about two male penguins at the Central Park Zoo who raised a chick together. Challengers cite LGBTQ themes. The book has no sexual content. It has appeared on challenged book lists in nearly every year since its 2005 publication.
Across the top ten
column2: the stated grounds cluster around sexual content and LGBTQ themes. The majority of these titles are either about LGBTQ people or written by LGBTQ authors. The pattern holds across the broader challenged titles list as well.

column1: What Happens to Library Directors

CB Factoid: The ALA Office for Intellectual Freedom documented at least five cases in 2022 and 2023 in which library directors were terminated or resigned under pressure following book challenge controversies
column2: including cases in Llano County
column3: Texas and Jamestown
column4: Michigan. Source: American Library Association
column5: Office for Intellectual Freedom
column6: ""Losing Our Librarians: A Report on Personnel Actions Related to Book Challenges

column1: Library boards operate with significant authority over library directors. When political pressure over book challenges reaches a board
column2: the director frequently becomes the pressure release valve. Documented cases show a consistent pattern. A community controversy erupts over specific titles. A library board
column3: facing political pressure from organized groups or elected officials
column4: votes to remove books that professional staff recommended retaining. When the director objects publicly or refuses to carry out removals
column5: the board may choose not to renew the director's contract
column6: eliminate the position
column7: or create conditions that lead to resignation.

column1: In Llano County
column2: Texas
column3: commissioners removed books over staff objections and eventually forced the closure of one library branch rather than restore challenged titles. The case produced federal litigation that continued into 2025. Library staff who resisted the removals left county employment.

column1: In Jamestown Township
column2: Michigan
column3: the community voted in 2022 to defund the local public library rather than have its board remove LGBTQ-themed books. The library director who had resisted removal requests became a central figure in the public conflict and faced sustained community pressure.

column1: These outcomes are documented and function as a signal to other library directors. The professional risk of publicly defending challenged materials against a politically motivated board is concrete and personal.
The Library Bill of Rights and the Gap
The
column2: including protection against censorship based on viewpoint."" target=""_blank"" rel=""noopener noreferrer"">ALA's Library Bill of Rights
column3: adopted in 1939 and amended several times since
column4: states that libraries should provide materials representing all points of view on current and historical issues and should resist efforts by groups to impose their own standards on the broader community. It explicitly addresses challenged materials
column5: stating that libraries should challenge censorship and that the right to read shall not be abridged.

column1: The Library Bill of Rights is a professional standard
column2: not a legal requirement. Library boards can disregard it. They frequently do. The gap between the policy framework that governs library professional practice and the political reality that governs library board decisions has always existed. The difference since 2021 is that the gap has widened and the consequences for individual librarians who try to close it have grown more severe.

column1: The OIF continues to track challenges and outcomes. The data remains dependent on voluntary reporting from people who sometimes face professional risk for reporting. The methodology is imperfect by design and by circumstance. What it shows
